вҳёпёҸ Kubernetes CKA вҖ” Certified Kubernetes Administrator

Complete Learning Guide with anihpj Django Project (jobpost app)

From complete beginner to CKA-certified Kubernetes administrator. Learn cluster architecture, installation, workloads, networking, storage, security, maintenance, and troubleshooting вҖ” all through hands-on examples with a real Django application.

рҹҹў Beginner Friendly вҳёпёҸ Kubernetes v1.31+ рҹҗҚ Django / Python рҹ“ҡ 37 Chapters вҸұпёҸ ~50 Hours рҹҺ“ CKA Exam вҖ” 100% Coverage рҹ“Ӣ 7 Appendices

рҹҡҖ Your CKA Learning Journey

рҹҺҜ Welcome! This guide takes you from complete beginner to CKA-certified Kubernetes administrator. Every concept is explained with real examples using the anihpj/jobpost Django project вҖ” you'll learn by doing, not just reading. Covers all CKA exam domains: Cluster Architecture (25%), Workloads & Scheduling (15%), Services & Networking (20%), Storage (10%), and Troubleshooting (30%).

рҹ“ҡ Complete Table of Contents

рҹ“ҡ рҹҹў Beginner

Understanding the Basics

What is Kubernetes, Docker & Containers, Architecture deep dive, Core Objects every admin must know.

Ch 1-4 ~7 hours
рҹӣ пёҸ рҹҹў Beginner

Part 2: Getting Started

Setting Up and Deploying

Environment setup, mastering kubectl, writing YAML manifests, and deploying anihpj to Kubernetes.

Ch 5-8 ~8 hours
рҹҸ—пёҸ рҹҹЎ CKA 25%

Part 3: Cluster Installation

Building Clusters from Scratch

kubeadm clusters, kubeconfig management, etcd operations, and control plane administration.

Ch 9-12 ~6 hours
вҡҷпёҸ рҹҹЎ CKA 15%

Part 4: Workloads & Scheduling

Managing Applications

Deployments, scheduling, resource management, Jobs, CronJobs, DaemonSets, and StatefulSets.

Ch 13-16 ~5 hours
рҹҢҗ рҹҹЎ CKA 20%

Part 5: Services & Networking

Connecting Everything

Networking model, Services, DNS, CoreDNS, Ingress controllers, and Network Policies.

Ch 17-20 ~5 hours
рҹ’ҫ рҹҹЎ CKA 10%

Part 6: Storage

Persistent Data

Volumes, PersistentVolumes, PersistentVolumeClaims, StorageClasses, ConfigMaps, and Secrets.

Ch 21-23 ~4 hours
рҹ”җ рҹҹЎ Intermediate

Part 7: Security

Securing the Cluster

RBAC, Service Accounts, TLS/PKI certificates, Pod Security, and Admission Controllers.

Ch 24-27 ~5 hours
рҹ”§ рҹ”ҙ Advanced

Part 8: Cluster Maintenance

Upgrades & Backups

Cluster upgrades with kubeadm, etcd backup and restore, node maintenance with drain and cordon.

Ch 28-30 ~4 hours
рҹ”Қ рҹ”ҙ CKA 30%

Part 9: Troubleshooting

Debug Like a Pro

Debug pods, nodes, cluster components, logging, monitoring, and CKA exam troubleshooting scenarios.

Ch 31-34 ~4 hours
рҹҡҖ рҹ”ҙ Advanced

Part 10: Production & Beyond

Enterprise Ready

HA clusters, managed Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS), CI/CD pipelines, GitOps with ArgoCD, and Helm charts.

Ch 35-37 ~4 hours

рҹҹў Part 1: Foundation Chapters 1-4

Chapter 1: What is Kubernetes? вҖ” Why Do Administrators Need It?

Before diving into cluster builds and config files, let's understand why Kubernetes exists, what problems it solves for operations teams, and why the CKA certification focuses on the platform itself rather than just deploying apps on it. We'll use our anihpj/jobpost Django app to make every concept concrete.

рҹҹў Beginner вҸұпёҸ ~1.5 hours рҹ“– Concepts рҹҺҜ Foundation

рҹҺҜ What You'll Learn

  • вң… The problems that exist before Kubernetes вҖ” manual scaling, 3 AM crashes, single points of failure
  • вң… What Kubernetes actually is вҖ” the simple, clear definition
  • вң… CKA vs CKAD perspective вҖ” why administrators own the cluster layer
  • вң… Kubernetes core concepts вҖ” clusters, control plane, worker nodes, pods, desired state
  • вң… The Kubernetes ecosystem вҖ” CNCF, CNI plugins, Helm, GitOps, service mesh
  • вң… How companies of different sizes use Kubernetes in real production
рҹҺ“ CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Foundation ALL DOMAINS
Understanding "Why" Matters on the CKA

The CKA is a performance-based exam (2 hours, 15-20 tasks in a live terminal). While Chapter 1 isn't directly tested, understanding why Kubernetes works the way it does helps you troubleshoot faster and reason through unfamiliar scenarios. Key concepts to internalize:

  • Desired State vs Actual State вҖ” K8s controllers constantly reconcile these. This explains why the control plane exists and why etcd is so critical.
  • Self-Healing вҖ” If a node goes down, K8s reschedules pods to healthy nodes. The CKA tests your ability to manage this.
  • Control Plane Components вҖ” Understanding what the API server, scheduler, controller-manager, and etcd do is directly tested on the CKA (25% of the exam).

1.1 The Problem Before Kubernetes

Imagine you've built anihpj вҖ” a Django app with a jobpost module where employers list jobs filtered by Skills, Location, and Author. It works perfectly on your laptop. But how do you run it in production so thousands of users can access it reliably?

рҹ“– Key Term: Container Orchestration

Container orchestration is the automated management of containerized applications across multiple machines вҖ” including deployment, scaling, networking, and availability. Kubernetes is the industry-standard container orchestrator, and as a CKA administrator, you are responsible for building and maintaining the orchestration platform itself.

The Evolution of Deployment

Software deployment has evolved through four major eras. Each solved problems of the previous one вҖ” but introduced new challenges:

рҹ–ҘпёҸ
2000s
Bare Metal

One app per physical server. No isolation. Massive resource waste. anihpj would need its own dedicated box.

вқҢ Wasteful & rigid
рҹ’»
2010s
Virtual Machines

Multiple VMs per server. Better isolation. Still heavy вҖ” each VM runs a full OS. Slow to provision.

вҡ пёҸ Heavy overhead
рҹ“Ұ
2015+
Containers

Lightweight, fast, shared kernel. But managing hundreds of containers manually across servers? Nightmare.

рҹҹЎ Almost there...
вҳёпёҸ
2018+
Kubernetes

Orchestrated containers. Auto-healing, auto-scaling, zero-downtime deploys. The admin builds and maintains this platform.

вң… Production-ready

The Scaling Problem вҖ” Before vs After

Without Kubernetes vs With Kubernetes
  вқҢ WITHOUT KUBERNETES                    вң… WITH KUBERNETES
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ            в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ        в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  рҹ–ҘпёҸ SERVER    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в”Ӯapp в”Ӯ в”Ӯapp в”Ӯ в”Ӯapp в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  anihpj      в”Ӯ  вҶҗ All в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  postgres    в”Ӯ  on    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ    вҶ‘ auto-heal & scale  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  nginx       в”Ӯ  ONE   в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ  box   в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  рҹ’Җ crash    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в”ӮNode 1в”Ӯ в”ӮNode 2в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  = TOTAL     в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    DOWNTIME  в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ в”Ӯрҹ“Ұв”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯрҹ“Ұв”Ӯ в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ        в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Manual restart at 3AM рҹҳұв”Ӯ            в”Ӯ  Self-healing 24/7 рҹҺү    в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ            в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Why Containers Alone Aren't Enough

Docker gives you a container. Kubernetes gives you an army of containers that manage themselves. Here's what you'd have to do manually without an orchestrator:

рҹҳ° Managing anihpj WITHOUT Kubernetes
  • рҹ”ҙ SSH into the server manually at 3 AM when the process crashes
  • рҹ”ҙ Restart the crashed Django process by hand вҖ” python manage.py runserver
  • рҹ”ҙ No load balancing вҖ” one server handles ALL traffic to /jobs/
  • рҹ”ҙ Deploying = copy files via SCP + pray nothing breaks
  • рҹ”ҙ Scaling = buy a new server, install everything from scratch, configure nginx
  • рҹ”ҙ Rolling back a bad deploy = restore from backup (if you remembered to make one)
рҹҺү Managing anihpj WITH Kubernetes
  • вң… Pod crashes at 3 AM вҶ’ K8s restarts it in seconds, you sleep through the night
  • вң… Traffic spikes вҶ’ K8s spins up more pods automatically via HPA
  • вң… Deploy new version вҶ’ K8s does a rolling update with zero downtime
  • вң… Bad deploy? вҶ’ kubectl rollout undo вҖ” instant rollback
  • вң… Node fails вҶ’ K8s migrates all pods to healthy nodes automatically
  • вң… Config changes? вҶ’ Update a ConfigMap, pods reload automatically
рҹ’Ў CKA Admin Perspective

As a CKA administrator, your job isn't just deploying apps вҖ” it's building and maintaining the platform that makes all of the above possible. You install the cluster, manage certificates, upgrade versions, backup etcd, and troubleshoot when things go wrong at the infrastructure level.

1.2 What is Kubernetes? (The Simple Answer)

рҹ“– Official Definition

"An open-source system for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications."

In plain English: Kubernetes is an operating system for your containers. You tell it what you want ("3 copies of anihpj, always running") вҖ” and it figures out how to make that happen across your cluster.

The Kubernetes Elevator Pitch

Declarative Management вҖ” You Declare, K8s Delivers
  рҹ“қ YOU DECLARE (YAML):             вҡҷпёҸ KUBERNETES ENSURES:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ         в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ replicas: 3           в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ вң… 3 containers running        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ image: anihpj:v2      в”Ӯ  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶  в”Ӯ вң… All using v2 image          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ memory: 256Mi         в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ вң… Each limited to 256Mi       в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ         в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  Pod crashes?     K8s restarts it automatically  рҹ”„
  Node dies?       K8s moves pods to other nodes  рҹ”Җ
  Traffic spikes?  K8s can auto-scale up          рҹ“Ҳ
  New version?     K8s rolls out with 0 downtime  рҹҡҖ

  рҹ’Ў You never say "start a container on node 2"
     You say "I want 3 copies" вҖ” K8s figures out the HOW

Quick Facts

вҳёпёҸ
Name Origin

K8s = K + 8 letters + s. Greek ОәП…ОІОөПҒОҪО®П„О·ПӮ means "helmsman" вҖ” steering the ship.

рҹҸӣпёҸ
Born at Google

Evolved from Google's internal Borg system. Open-sourced in 2014, donated to CNCF.

рҹ”Ө
Written in Go

Built with Google's Go language for high concurrency, compiled performance, and clean APIs.

рҹ“ң
Apache 2.0 License

Fully open-source. Free to use, modify, and distribute commercially.

Kubernetes Lineage вҖ” From Borg to K8s
  Google Borg (2003)           Google Omega (2013)          Kubernetes (2014)
 в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
 в”Ӯ Internal cluster     в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Next-gen scheduler  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Open-source for     в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ manager at Google    в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ research project    в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ everyone! вҳёпёҸ         в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ Ran Gmail, Search,   в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ Flexible queuing,   в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ CNCF graduated 2018 в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ YouTube, Maps        в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ multi-scheduler     в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ Largest OSS after   в”Ӯ
 в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ     в”Ӯ Linux kernel        в”Ӯ
                                                          в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Kubernetes by the Numbers

CNCF Survey вҖ” K8s Adoption Rate 96%
Fortune 100 Companies Using K8s 77%
GitHub Stars (Top 10 OSS Projects) 112K+
Active GitHub Contributors 3,500+

1.3 The CKA Perspective: Administrator vs Developer

There are two Kubernetes certifications from the Linux Foundation вҖ” and they test fundamentally different skills. Understanding the boundary between them is crucial for knowing what to study.

рҹ‘ЁвҖҚрҹ’»
CKAD вҖ” Developer

"I deploy and configure apps on a cluster someone else built."

  • Create Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps
  • Configure health checks and resource limits
  • Build multi-container pods
  • Manage application lifecycle
рҹ”§
CKA вҖ” Administrator вҶҗ YOU

"I build, manage, and troubleshoot the cluster itself."

  • Install clusters with kubeadm
  • Upgrade cluster versions safely
  • Backup & restore etcd
  • Manage certificates & RBAC
  • Troubleshoot broken nodes
  • Configure networking (CNI, DNS)
CKA Administrator Responsibilities Map
 в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
 в”Ӯ  THE K8s ADMINISTRATOR OWNS EVERYTHING BELOW THE APP       в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ                                                            в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  рҹ‘ЁвҖҚрҹ’» Developer's World (CKAD)                         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  рҹ”§ Administrator's World (CKA) вҶҗ YOU                в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ вҡҷпёҸ  Cluster Installation (kubeadm)              в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ”җ Certificate Management (PKI/TLS)            в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ’ҫ etcd Backup & Restore                       в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ в¬ҶпёҸ  Cluster Upgrades (version management)       в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ–ҘпёҸ  Node Management (drain, cordon, uncordon)   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹӣЎпёҸ  RBAC & Security Policies                   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹҢҗ Networking (CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy)       в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ’ҝ Storage (PV, PVC, StorageClasses)            в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ”Қ Troubleshooting (nodes, pods, cluster)      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹҸ—пёҸ  High Availability & Disaster Recovery       в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
 в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

CKA Exam Domain Weights

The CKA exam tests five domains. As a CKA candidate, you need to know exactly where each topic fits:

Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25%
Workloads & Scheduling 15%
Services & Networking 20%
Storage 10%
Troubleshooting 30%
вҡ пёҸ Troubleshooting is 30% of the CKA!

Unlike the CKAD (which focuses on building apps), the CKA dedicates 30% of the exam to troubleshooting broken clusters, failed nodes, crashed control plane components, and networking issues. You must know how to diagnose and fix problems under time pressure.

Aspect CKAD (Developer) CKA (Administrator) вҶҗ YOU
Focus Application deployment & configuration Cluster lifecycle & infrastructure
Duration 2 hours, ~15-20 tasks 2 hours, ~15-20 tasks
Passing Score 66% 66%
etcd Not tested Backup, restore, snapshot
kubeadm Not tested Install, upgrade, configure
Certificates Not tested CSR, TLS, certificate rotation
Node Management Not tested Drain, cordon, troubleshoot
Networking Services, Ingress basics CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, NetworkPolicy

CKA Exam Logistics вҖ” Quick Reference

рҹ“Ӣ
Exam Format
  • Duration: 2 hours
  • Questions: 15-20 performance tasks
  • Passing: 66%
  • Environment: Linux terminal (browser-based)
  • K8s version: Latest stable (check CNCF site)
  • Retake: 1 free retake included
рҹ“ҡ
Allowed Resources
  • вң… kubernetes.io/docs вҖ” official docs
  • вң… kubernetes.io/blog вҖ” official blog
  • вң… github.com/kubernetes вҖ” source code
  • вқҢ No other websites
  • вқҢ No notes, bookmarks, or AI tools
  • рҹ’Ў Tip: Practice navigating docs fast!

Kubernetes Release Cycle

K8s releases 3 minor versions per year (~every 4 months). As a CKA admin, you manage cluster upgrades and must understand the support window:

рҹҶ•
Month 0
New Release

e.g., v1.32 releases. Includes new features, deprecations, and bug fixes.

вң… Latest
рҹӣЎпёҸ
0-14 months
Supported

Receives patch releases (security/bug fixes). CKA tests within this window.

вң… Patch support
вҡ пёҸ
14+ months
End of Life

No more patches. Running EOL versions = security risk. Must upgrade.

вқҢ Unsupported
рҹ’Ў CKA Upgrade Rule: One Minor Version at a Time

Kubernetes only supports upgrading one minor version at a time (e.g., 1.30 вҶ’ 1.31 вҶ’ 1.32). You cannot skip versions. The CKA tests this: you'll be asked to upgrade a cluster from, say, 1.31 to 1.32 using kubeadm upgrade. Attempting to jump from 1.30 to 1.32 will fail.

1.4 Kubernetes Core Concepts вҖ” The Big Picture

Before getting hands-on, you need to understand five fundamental concepts that everything else in Kubernetes builds upon. These form the mental model you'll use for the entire CKA journey.

рҹҸ—пёҸ
Cluster

A set of machines (nodes) managed by Kubernetes as a single unit.

рҹ§ 
Control Plane

The "brain" that makes all scheduling & orchestration decisions.

рҹ’Ә
Worker Nodes

The "muscles" that actually run your containerized applications.

рҹ“Ұ
Pod

Smallest deployable unit вҖ” one or more containers sharing network & storage.

рҹҺҜ
Desired State

You declare what you want вҖ” K8s continuously ensures it stays that way.

The Kubernetes Architecture вҖ” Bird's Eye View

Complete Cluster Architecture
 в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ                                      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ                   kubelet / kube-proxy                           в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ                                      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ              рҹ’Ә WORKER NODES ("The Muscles")               в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ

 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯрҹ“Ұ Pod     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯрҹ“Ұ Pod     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ anihpj   в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ anihpj   в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ :8000    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ :8000    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯрҹ“Ұ Pod     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯрҹ“Ұ Pod     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ postgres в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ redis    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ :5432    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ :6379    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ         в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  You say: "I want 3 anihpj pods" (Desired State)               в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  K8s ensures: 3 pods are ALWAYS running (Actual State)         в”Ӯ
 в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Control Plane Components вҖ” The Admin Must Know These

рҹ§  Control Plane Components (CKA Exam: 25%)

рҹ‘ЁвҖҚвңҲпёҸ kube-apiserver
The front door вҖ” ALL communication goes through the API server. kubectl commands, node heartbeats, controller actions. If this goes down, the cluster is blind.
рҹ“Ӣ etcd
The cluster's brain memory вҖ” a distributed key-value store holding ALL cluster state. CKA tests etcd backup & restore. Lose etcd without a backup = lose your entire cluster.
рҹҺҜ kube-scheduler
The matchmaker вҖ” watches for new Pods with no node assigned, then picks the best node based on resources, affinity, taints/tolerations.
рҹ”„ kube-controller-manager
The watchdog вҖ” runs controller loops that continuously compare desired state vs actual state and take corrective action (e.g., "only 2 of 3 replicas running вҶ’ create 1 more").
вҳҒпёҸ cloud-controller-manager
Cloud glue (optional) вҖ” integrates with AWS/Azure/GCP APIs for load balancers, persistent disks, and node lifecycle. Not present in bare-metal clusters.

Worker Node Components

рҹ’Ә Worker Node Components

рҹӨ– kubelet
The node agent вҖ” receives pod specs from the API server and ensures the containers are running and healthy on that node. Reports node status back.
рҹ”Җ kube-proxy
The network plumber вҖ” maintains network rules on each node so pods can communicate with each other and with external traffic via Services.
рҹҗӢ Container Runtime
The engine вҖ” actually runs containers. Kubernetes supports containerd (default), CRI-O, or any CRI-compatible runtime. Docker was removed in v1.24.

Desired State vs Actual State вҖ” The Declarative Magic

The Reconciliation Loop
  рҹ“қ YOU DECLARE (YAML):           вҡҷпёҸ KUBERNETES ENSURES:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ        в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ replicas: 3          в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ рҹ“Ұ рҹ“Ұ  running             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ image: anihpj:v2     в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ в”Ӯ all v2 images                в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ memory: 256Mi        в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ each вүӨ 256Mi                  в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                                            в”Ӯ
                                   Controller Loop рҹ”„
                                            в”Ӯ
                              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                              в”Ӯ Pod crashes?  вҶ’ Restart it  в”Ӯ
                              в”Ӯ Node dies?    вҶ’ Reschedule  в”Ӯ
                              в”Ӯ Too few pods? вҶ’ Create more в”Ӯ
                              в”Ӯ Too many?     вҶ’ Terminate   в”Ӯ
                              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ’Ў Why Declarative Matters for CKA

The CKA exam expects you to work declaratively (YAML manifests) rather than imperatively. You declare the desired state, apply it with kubectl apply -f, and trust Kubernetes controllers to make it happen. Understanding this model is essential for troubleshooting вҖ” when something breaks, you compare desired state (in etcd) against actual state (on nodes).

1.5 The Kubernetes Ecosystem

Kubernetes doesn't work alone. It sits at the center of a massive ecosystem of tools, plugins, and platforms вҖ” all governed by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). As a CKA administrator, you'll work with many of these tools daily.

Kubernetes Ecosystem Orbit
                         Helm рҹ“Ұ (Package Manager)
                    в•ұ                               в•І
              ArgoCD рҹ”„                         Calico рҹҢҗ
            (GitOps)   в•І                      в•ұ  (CNI)
                        в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
               в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ   KUBERNETES    в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Prometheus рҹ“Ҡ
                        в”Ӯ     вҳёпёҸ Core      в”Ӯ        (Monitoring)
                        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
            containerd рҹҗӢв•ұ                     в•І  Istio рҹ•ёпёҸ
            (Runtime)                           (Service Mesh)
                    в•І                               в•ұ
                        Grafana рҹ“Ҳ            Cilium рҹ”’
                      (Dashboards)          (eBPF Networking)

              All governed by CNCF (Cloud Native Computing Foundation)

Key Ecosystem Categories for CKA

рҹҗӢ
Container Runtimes

containerd (default since K8s 1.24), CRI-O (Red Hat). Docker engine was deprecated вҖ” Kubernetes uses the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) directly.

рҹҢҗ
CNI Plugins (Networking)

Calico (network policies), Cilium (eBPF-based), Flannel (simple overlay), Weave. CKA requires you to install a CNI plugin during cluster setup.

рҹ“Ұ
Package Management

Helm вҖ” the "apt/yum for Kubernetes." Packages K8s manifests into reusable charts. Essential for installing complex applications like Prometheus, cert-manager, ingress controllers.

рҹ”„
GitOps

ArgoCD, FluxCD вҖ” automatically sync cluster state from a Git repository. Changes in Git trigger deployments. The source of truth lives in version control.

рҹ•ёпёҸ
Service Mesh

Istio, Linkerd вҖ” adds observability, traffic management, and mTLS encryption between services without changing application code.

рҹ“Ҡ
Monitoring & Observability

Prometheus (metrics), Grafana (dashboards), Loki (logs), Jaeger (tracing). The CKA expects you to know kubectl top and metrics-server.

рҹ“– CNCF вҖ” Who Governs Kubernetes?

The Cloud Native Computing Foundation (part of the Linux Foundation) governs Kubernetes and 170+ related projects. Projects go through three maturity levels: Sandbox вҶ’ Incubating вҶ’ Graduated. Kubernetes was the first project to graduate (2018). CNCF also manages the CKA and CKAD certifications.

The CNCF Landscape вҖ” Maturity Levels

рҹҺ“ Graduated Production-proven, widely adopted
Kubernetes Prometheus containerd CoreDNS etcd Helm
рҹ§Ә Incubating Growing adoption, maturing features
ArgoCD Cilium Knative Kyverno Backstage
рҹҢұ Sandbox Early-stage, experimental projects
Headlamp OpenCost Inspektor Gadget

1.6 How Companies Use Kubernetes in Real Life

Kubernetes isn't just for tech giants. Companies of every size use it вҖ” the scale of their setup and admin tasks varies dramatically.

Company Size K8s Setup Admin Tasks Team
рҹҸ  Startup (5-20 people)
e.g., anihpj		 1 cluster, 3 nodes. Start with Minikube, graduate to managed cloud (EKS/AKS/GKE). Deploy apps, monitor health, basic troubleshooting, set up CI/CD. 1 admin does it all
рҹҸў Mid-size (50-200 people) 2-5 clusters. Separate dev/staging/prod. Multi-team RBAC. CI/CD pipelines, RBAC, cluster upgrades, monitoring, cost optimization, security policies. 2-4 admins
рҹҸҷпёҸ Enterprise (500+ people) 10-100+ clusters. Multi-region, HA, service mesh, custom networking. HA & disaster recovery, security compliance, GitOps, multi-cluster management, platform engineering. Dedicated platform team (5-20+)

A Day in the Life of a Kubernetes Administrator

1
Morning Check

Review monitoring dashboards (Grafana/Prometheus). Check node health, pod restarts, resource usage. Look for alerts triggered overnight.

2
Troubleshooting

Investigate any failed pods or node issues. kubectl describe node, kubectl logs, check system journals with journalctl -u kubelet.

3
Planned Changes

Apply planned upgrades, RBAC changes, or new namespace configurations. Always test in staging first, then promote to production.

4
Security & Compliance

Review recent audit logs, rotate certificates if due, verify NetworkPolicies, check RBAC for over-permissioned accounts.

5
Capacity Planning

Monitor resource trends. Are nodes nearing capacity? Do we need to add nodes or right-size pod requests/limits?

Real Companies Running Kubernetes at Scale

рҹҺө
Spotify

Migrated 150+ services to K8s. Reduced deployment time from hours to minutes. Custom platform built on top of K8s.

рҹӣ’
Shopify

Handles Black Friday traffic (millions of requests/sec) on K8s clusters that auto-scale to handle 10x normal load.

рҹҺ®
Pokemon GO

Launched on GKE. Scaled from expected 1x traffic to 50x in days. K8s handled the scaling that would have crashed traditional infrastructure.

рҹҸҰ
Capital One

Runs critical banking services on K8s with strict compliance requirements. Proves K8s works in highly regulated industries.

рҹ’Ў Where You Fit In вҖ” The anihpj Journey

Throughout this guide, we'll follow the journey of anihpj вҖ” our Django job board application вҖ” from a single-server deployment to a production-grade Kubernetes cluster. You'll learn to build the infrastructure that makes reliable, scalable deployments possible. By the end, you'll have the skills to pass the CKA exam and administer real-world clusters.

вҡ пёҸ CKA Gotcha: Conceptual Traps from Chapter 1

  • Kubernetes вү  Docker вҖ” K8s is the orchestrator; containerd/CRI-O is the runtime. Docker as a runtime was removed in v1.24
  • Desired State вү  Current State вҖ” The exam tests whether you understand the reconciliation loop: controllers constantly compare desired vs actual, and self-heal
  • Control Plane вү  Master Node вҖ” Modern K8s avoids the term "master." The CKA uses "control plane node." Know the four components: API Server, Scheduler, Controller Manager, etcd
  • Pods вү  Containers вҖ” A Pod can contain multiple containers sharing the same network namespace. On the exam, you'll see sidecar and init container patterns

Chapter 1 CKA Quick Wins: When asked about architecture on the exam, remember: kube-apiserver вҶ’ front door, etcd вҶ’ brain, kube-scheduler вҶ’ which-node, kube-controller-manager вҶ’ reconciliation. All run as static pods in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/.

Chapter 2: Docker and Containers вҖ” The Building Blocks

Learn containers, Docker basics, and container runtimes вҖ” the foundation Kubernetes builds on. As a CKA administrator, you must understand how containers work because your cluster runs them.

рҹҹў Beginner вҸұпёҸ ~2 hours рҹ“– Hands-On рҹҗі Docker

рҹҺҜ What You'll Learn

  • вң… Why containers solve the "works on my machine" problem
  • вң… Containers vs Virtual Machines вҖ” when to use each
  • вң… Docker basics: Dockerfile, images, containers, registries
  • вң… Building a Docker image for the anihpj Django app
  • вң… Essential Docker commands every admin needs
  • вң… Container runtimes: containerd, CRI-O, and why Docker was removed from K8s
рҹҺ“ CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Troubleshooting 30%
Container Runtime Knowledge on the CKA

The CKA doesn't test Docker commands directly, but you must understand container runtimes because:

  • Cluster installation вҖ” you configure containerd/CRI-O as part of kubeadm setup
  • Troubleshooting вҖ” when pods fail, you may need to check runtime logs with crictl
  • Node issues вҖ” container runtime failures cause node NotReady status
  • Image management вҖ” understanding image layers helps debug ImagePullBackOff errors

2.1 Why Containers? The "Works on My Machine" Problem

Every developer has lived this nightmare: the app works perfectly on your laptop, but crashes in production. Containers solve this by packaging everything the app needs into a single portable unit.

The "Works on My Machine" Problem
  рҹ‘©вҖҚрҹ’» Developer laptop:  "anihpj works perfectly!"
  рҹ–ҘпёҸ Production server:  "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'django'" рҹ’Җ

  WHY? Different Python version, missing packages,
  different OS, missing environment variables...

  SOLUTION: Pack EVERYTHING into a container рҹ“Ұ
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ Docker Container                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Python 3.12                     в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Django 5.0                      в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ gunicorn, psycopg2              в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ anihpj source code              в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ settings & config files         в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Runs IDENTICALLY everywhere         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Laptop вң…  Staging вң…  Production вң… в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ“– Key Term: Container

A container is a lightweight, standalone, executable package that includes everything needed to run a piece of software: code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings. Containers share the host's OS kernel, making them much lighter than virtual machines.

рҹ“Ұ
Portable

Same container runs on Linux, Mac, Windows, cloud, bare metal вҖ” identical behavior everywhere.

рҹ”’
Isolated

Each container has its own filesystem, network, and process space. No conflicts between apps.

вҡЎ
Fast

Start in seconds (not minutes like VMs). Share host kernel вҖ” no full OS boot needed.

рҹ“җ
Lightweight

Megabytes (not gigabytes). Run dozens of containers on a single machine.

2.2 Containers vs Virtual Machines

Both containers and VMs provide isolation, but they work at fundamentally different levels. Understanding this helps you troubleshoot as a CKA admin.

VM Architecture vs Container Architecture
  VIRTUAL MACHINES                    CONTAINERS
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ            в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”ӮApp Aв”Ӯ  в”ӮApp Bв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”ӮApp Aв”Ӯ  в”ӮApp Bв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”ӮLibs в”Ӯ  в”ӮLibs в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”ӮLibs в”Ӯ  в”ӮLibs в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”ӮGuestв”Ӯ  в”ӮGuestв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ Shared  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ OS  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ OS  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”ӮContainer Eng.в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (containerd) в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ   Hypervisor    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”Ӯ    Host OS       в”Ӯв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ    Host OS      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в”Ӯ    Hardware      в”Ӯв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ    Hardware     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Heavy: GB, minutes   в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ Light: MB, seconds  в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ            в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Feature Virtual Machine Container
Size Gigabytes (full OS per VM) Megabytes (shared kernel)
Startup Time Minutes (OS boot required) Seconds (process start)
OS Full guest OS per VM Shares host kernel
Isolation Strong (hardware-level) Process-level (namespaces/cgroups)
Performance Near-native (hypervisor overhead) Native (direct kernel access)
Density ~10-20 VMs per server ~100-1000 containers per server
Best For Different OS needed, strong isolation Same OS, many microservices
рҹ’Ў CKA Admin Note: Kubernetes Nodes ARE VMs (Usually)

In most real-world clusters, Kubernetes worker nodes are virtual machines running in a cloud provider (EC2, Azure VM, GCE). So you actually use both: VMs as the host infrastructure, and containers for the workloads. The Kubernetes admin manages both layers.

2.3 Docker Basics for Kubernetes Admins

Docker established the container ecosystem. Even though Kubernetes no longer uses Docker as a runtime, Docker's concepts and tooling remain fundamental to the workflow.

рҹ“қDockerfile
вҶ’
рҹ”Ёdocker build
вҶ’
рҹ“ҖImage
вҶ’
рҹҡҖdocker run
вҶ’
рҹ“ҰContainer
рҹ“қ
Dockerfile

A text recipe defining how to build an image. Lists the base OS, dependencies, code, and startup command.

рҹ“Җ
Image

The built, read-only template. Like a class in OOP вҖ” it defines the container but isn't running yet.

рҹ“Ұ
Container

A running instance of an image. Like an object created from a class вҖ” it's live and executing.

рҹҸӘ
Registry

Image storage & distribution. Docker Hub (public), ECR (AWS), GCR (GCP), ACR (Azure).

Image Layers вҖ” How Docker Images Work

Docker Image Layer Architecture
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  Container Layer (read-write)         в”Ӯ вҶҗ Runtime changes
  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
  в”Ӯ  Layer 5: CMD ["gunicorn", "..."]    в”Ӯ вҶҗ Startup command
  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
  в”Ӯ  Layer 4: COPY . /app               в”Ӯ вҶҗ anihpj source code
  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
  в”Ӯ  Layer 3: RUN pip install -r req.txt в”Ӯ вҶҗ Python packages
  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
  в”Ӯ  Layer 2: COPY requirements.txt .    в”Ӯ вҶҗ Dependencies file
  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
  в”Ӯ  Layer 1: FROM python:3.12-slim      в”Ӯ вҶҗ Base OS + Python
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
  
  рҹ’Ў Each layer is cached. Change Layer 4 вҶ’ only rebuild 4 & 5.
     This is why you copy requirements.txt BEFORE source code!
рҹ“– Why Layer Order Matters for CKA

When troubleshooting ImagePullBackOff errors, understanding image layers helps you diagnose whether the issue is a missing base image, a build failure, or a registry authentication problem. As an admin, you configure imagePullSecrets for private registries.

2.4 Building an anihpj Docker Image

Let's build a production-ready Docker image for our anihpj Django application. This is the image Kubernetes will pull and run as pods.

Dockerfile 
# Stage 1: Base image with Python
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends gcc libpq-dev && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements first (for layer caching)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy project source code
COPY . .

# Collect static files
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# Create non-root user (security best practice)
RUN addgroup --system appgroup && \
    adduser --system --ingroup appgroup appuser
USER appuser

# Expose port
EXPOSE 8000

# Run with gunicorn (production WSGI server)
CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", \
     "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", \
     "--workers", "3"]
.dockerignore 
# Don't copy these into the image
__pycache__/
*.pyc
*.pyo
.git/
.gitignore
.env
db.sqlite3
venv/
env/
node_modules/
*.md
.dockerignore
Dockerfile
вҡ пёҸ Security: Always Run as Non-Root

Notice the USER appuser directive. Running containers as root is a security risk вҖ” if an attacker breaks out of the container, they'd have root access to the host. CKA-level admins enforce this via PodSecurityStandards and security contexts.

Build & Test Workflow

terminal вҖ” build & run 
# Build the image
docker build -t anihpj:v1 .

# Run the container
docker run -d -p 8000:8000 --name anihpj-app anihpj:v1

# Test it
curl http://localhost:8000/jobs/

# View logs
docker logs anihpj-app

# Push to registry (for Kubernetes to pull later)
docker tag anihpj:v1 myregistry/anihpj:v1
docker push myregistry/anihpj:v1

2.5 Essential Docker Commands for Admins

While the CKA doesn't test Docker directly, these commands help you build images and troubleshoot container issues during daily admin work.

Command What It Does CKA Relevance
docker build -t anihpj:v1 . Build image from Dockerfile Prep images for K8s
docker run -p 8000:8000 anihpj Run container, expose port Test before deploying
docker ps List running containers Debug outside K8s
docker logs <id> View container logs Similar to kubectl logs
docker exec -it <id> bash Shell into container Similar to kubectl exec
docker images List local images Check cached images
docker push <registry>/anihpj Push image to registry Required for K8s to pull
docker pull <image> Pull image from registry Pre-cache on nodes
docker stop <id> Stop a running container Graceful shutdown
docker system prune Clean up unused resources Disk space on nodes
рҹ’Ў On the CKA Exam: Use crictl, Not Docker

On CKA exam nodes, Docker may not be installed. Use crictl (CRI CLI) instead:

  • crictl ps вҖ” list containers (like docker ps)
  • crictl logs <id> вҖ” view logs (like docker logs)
  • crictl images вҖ” list images (like docker images)
  • crictl inspect <id> вҖ” container details (like docker inspect)

2.6 Container Runtimes: Beyond Docker

Since Kubernetes v1.24, Docker is no longer a supported container runtime. Kubernetes uses the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) to communicate with runtimes directly. As a CKA admin, you install and configure the runtime on every node.

Container Runtime Evolution in Kubernetes
  Before v1.24:                   After v1.24:
  
  kubelet                         kubelet
     в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ
     в–ј                               в–ј
  dockershim (REMOVED вқҢ)         CRI interface
     в”Ӯ                            в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
     в–ј                            в–ј       в–ј
  Docker daemon               containerd   CRI-O
     в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
     в–ј                            в–ј         в–ј
  containerd                  containers  containers
     в”Ӯ
     в–ј                        (direct вҖ” no Docker overhead!)
  containers
                              
  рҹ’Ў Docker images still work! Only the runtime changed.
     You still USE Docker to BUILD images on your laptop.
рҹҗӢ
containerd

The industry standard. Extracted from Docker itself. Used by GKE, EKS, AKS, and most managed K8s providers. Lightweight, stable, CNCF graduated project.

  • Default runtime for kubeadm clusters
  • CLI tool: ctr (low-level), crictl (K8s-native)
  • Config: /etc/containerd/config.toml
рҹ”ҙ
CRI-O

Purpose-built for Kubernetes вҖ” nothing more, nothing less. Preferred by Red Hat/OpenShift. Minimal footprint, K8s version-aligned releases.

  • Used by OpenShift and Fedora CoreOS
  • Version-locked to Kubernetes versions
  • Config: /etc/crio/crio.conf
вҡ пёҸ CKA Exam: You May Need to Configure the Container Runtime

If a CKA task involves setting up a new node or troubleshooting a node that's NotReady, check the container runtime first:

terminal вҖ” runtime troubleshooting 
# Check if containerd is running
systemctl status containerd

# Check kubelet logs for CRI errors
journalctl -u kubelet | grep -i "container runtime"

# List containers via CRI
crictl ps

# Check runtime endpoint
crictl info

# Restart containerd if stuck
sudo systemctl restart containerd

рҹ”‘ Chapter 2 Key Takeaways for CKA

Containers are the workloads your cluster runs. As a CKA admin, you don't write Dockerfiles daily вҖ” but you must understand container images, runtimes, and how Kubernetes interacts with them via CRI. When a pod fails to start, your troubleshooting starts at the runtime level.

Multi-Stage Build вҖ” Production Pattern

Multi-stage builds produce smaller, more secure images by separating the build environment from the final runtime. Here's how to apply this to anihpj:

Dockerfile.production вҖ” multi-stage build 
# Stage 1: Builder вҖ” install dependencies
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

# Stage 2: Runtime вҖ” final slim image
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
COPY . .
RUN addgroup --system app && adduser --system --ingroup app app
USER app
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
Multi-Stage Build: Size Reduction
  Single-stage:    python:3.12   + gcc + build tools + app
                   ~900 MB рҹҳ¬

  Multi-stage:     python:3.12-slim + app (no build tools!)
                   ~150 MB вң…

  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ Stage 1: Builder   в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ Stage 2: Runtime   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ gcc, build-deps    в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ ONLY runtime deps  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ pip install        в”Ӯcopy в”Ӯ + app code         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ (discarded after!) в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ FINAL IMAGE        в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Container Image Security Checklist

рҹӣЎпёҸ
Do These вң…
  • Use slim/distroless base images
  • Run as non-root (USER appuser)
  • Pin image versions (python:3.12.3 not :latest)
  • Scan images with Trivy or Snyk
  • Use .dockerignore to exclude secrets
  • Set readOnlyRootFilesystem: true in K8s
рҹҡ«
Avoid These вқҢ
  • Running as root in containers
  • Using :latest tag (unpredictable)
  • Embedding secrets in images
  • Using full OS base images (Ubuntu 1GB+)
  • Leaving build tools in production images
  • Skipping image vulnerability scans

вҡ пёҸ CKA Gotcha: Container & Image Traps

  • containerd vs Docker вҖ” Kubernetes removed dockershim in v1.24. The CKA expects you to know that crictl (not docker) is the CLI for inspecting containers on nodes
  • Image pull policies вҖ” :latest defaults to Always; tagged images default to IfNotPresent. This can cause unexpected pulls on exam clusters
  • Multi-stage builds вҖ” You won't build images on the CKA, but you WILL need to understand image references (registry/repo:tag) for troubleshooting failed pulls
  • ImagePullBackOff вҖ” Common exam scenario. Check: wrong image name, missing tag, private registry without imagePullSecrets, or network issues

CKA Speed Tip: To quickly debug image issues: kubectl describe pod <name> | grep -A5 "Events". The Events section reveals whether it's an image pull failure, OOM kill, or crash loop.

вҡЎ Imperative equivalents for container troubleshooting: crictl ps вҖ” list running containers on a node
crictl images вҖ” list images cached on a node
crictl logs <container-id> вҖ” view container logs directly via CRI

Chapter 3: Kubernetes Architecture вҖ” The Admin's Blueprint

Deep dive into control plane, worker nodes, and how pods get created вҖ” the administrator's perspective. This chapter maps directly to the CKA exam's "Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration" domain (25%).

рҹҹў Beginner вҸұпёҸ ~2 hours рҹ“– Architecture рҹҺҜ CKA Core

рҹҺҜ What You'll Learn

  • вң… Complete cluster architecture вҖ” control plane + worker nodes
  • вң… Every control plane component: API server, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager
  • вң… Every worker node component: kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime
  • вң… How a Pod gets created вҖ” the full 8-step journey
  • вң… Cluster networking overview вҖ” CNI, pod network, service network
  • вң… Static Pods вҖ” admin-only concept used by kubeadm
рҹҺ“ CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Troubleshooting 30%
Architecture Knowledge is CKA Gold

Every CKA troubleshooting question requires you to understand how components interact. When a node is NotReady, you check kubelet. When pods aren't scheduling, you check the scheduler. When state is lost, you restore etcd. This chapter is the foundation for 55% of the exam.

  • API Server вҖ” diagnosing connectivity and authentication issues
  • etcd вҖ” backup, restore, and snapshot operations (tested directly)
  • Scheduler вҖ” understanding why pods are Pending
  • Static Pods вҖ” managing control plane components via kubelet

3.1 Cluster Architecture Overview for Admins

A Kubernetes cluster consists of two layers: the Control Plane (brain) that makes decisions, and Worker Nodes (muscles) that run workloads. As a CKA administrator, you install, configure, and maintain both layers.

Complete Kubernetes Cluster Architecture
 в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
 в”Ӯ  KUBERNETES CLUSTER                                              в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ                                                                  в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ CONTROL PLANE в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ API      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ etcd      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ kube-scheduler   в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Server   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (database)в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (pod placement)  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹҡӘ Front в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ§  Brain  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ӣ "Pod вҶ’ Node"  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ door for в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ stores    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ picks best node  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ ALL cmds в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ all state в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ for each pod     в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ controller-manager в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ cloud-controller-mgr  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ”„ Watches state   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ вҳҒпёҸ Cloud integration   в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ & corrects drift   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ LB, routes, disks     в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ API                                    в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ                   WORKER NODES                         в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Worker Node 1     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ Worker Node 2     в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ                   в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ                   в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹӨ– kubelet        в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ рҹӨ– kubelet        в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹҢҗ kube-proxy     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ рҹҢҗ kube-proxy     в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹҗӢ containerd     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ рҹҗӢ containerd     в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ                   в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ                   в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
 в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ      в”Ӯ
 в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ§  Control Plane Makes all orchestration decisions
kube-apiserver etcd kube-scheduler controller-manager
рҹ’Ә Worker Nodes Run the actual workloads
kubelet kube-proxy containerd/CRI-O Pods
рҹҢҗ Networking Layer Connects everything together
CNI Plugin CoreDNS Service Network Pod Network

3.2 Control Plane Components вҖ” Deep Dive for Admins

These are the components you maintain, troubleshoot, and restore as a CKA administrator. Each one is critical вҖ” if any fails, the cluster is impacted.

a) kube-apiserver вҖ” The Front Door

рҹ‘ЁвҖҚвңҲпёҸ kube-apiserver

рҹҺҜ Role
The ONLY entry point to the cluster. ALL communication (kubectl, kubelet, controllers) goes through the API server.
рҹ”җ Security
Handles authentication (certificates, tokens), authorization (RBAC), and admission control (validation, mutation).
рҹ“Ӯ Config
Static pod manifest: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
рҹ”Ң Default Port
6443 (secure HTTPS)
рҹ’Җ If It Fails
No kubectl commands work, no new pods are created, nodes can't report status. Existing pods keep running (kubelet is independent).

b) etcd вҖ” The Cluster Database

рҹ“Ӣ etcd вҖ” The Brain's Memory

рҹҺҜ Role
Distributed key-value store holding ALL cluster state: pods, services, secrets, config, RBAC rules вҖ” everything.
рҹ”„ Consensus
Uses Raft protocol. Requires a quorum (majority) of nodes to agree on writes. That's why HA clusters use 3 or 5 etcd instances.
рҹ”Ң Ports
2379 (client), 2380 (peer communication)
рҹ“қ CKA Exam
Backup & restore is directly tested. You must know etcdctl snapshot save and etcdctl snapshot restore.
рҹҡЁ WARNING: etcd Lost = Cluster Lost

If etcd data is lost without a backup, your entire cluster configuration is gone. No pods, no services, no secrets вҖ” nothing. Always maintain regular etcd backups. This is why the CKA tests this skill.

terminal вҖ” etcd backup (CKA exam skill) 
# Backup etcd
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Verify backup
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup.db --write-table

c) kube-scheduler вҖ” The Matchmaker

рҹҺҜ kube-scheduler

рҹҺҜ Role
Watches for new Pods with no node assigned, evaluates all available nodes, and assigns the Pod to the best fit.
рҹ”Қ Process
Filter (eliminate unfit nodes) вҶ’ Score (rank remaining) вҶ’ Bind (assign to top-scored node).
рҹ“Ҡ Considers
CPU/memory availability, nodeSelector, affinity/anti-affinity, taints/tolerations, PV requirements, topology spread.
рҹ’Җ If It Fails
New pods stay in Pending state. Existing pods keep running. Check with: kubectl get pods -A | grep Pending
1
Watch

Scheduler watches API server for new Pods with nodeName: ""

2
Filter

Eliminate nodes that can't run the Pod (insufficient resources, taints, affinity rules)

3
Score

Rank remaining nodes by fit (spread, resource balance, affinity preferences)

4
Bind

Assign Pod to top-scored node вҶ’ update etcd вҶ’ kubelet takes over

d) kube-controller-manager вҖ” The Watchdog

рҹ”„ kube-controller-manager

рҹҺҜ Role
Runs multiple controller loops in a single process. Each controller watches one resource type and reconciles desired state vs actual state.
рҹ”Қ Node Controller
Monitors node heartbeats. Marks nodes as NotReady after 40s of no heartbeat. Evicts pods after 5 minutes.
рҹ“Ұ ReplicaSet Ctrl
Maintains correct pod count. Spec says 3 replicas but only 2 running? Creates 1 more.
рҹ”— Endpoint Ctrl
Connects Services to Pods by maintaining the Endpoints object (lists of pod IPs matching the service selector).
рҹ‘Ө SA Controller
Creates default ServiceAccount for each new namespace.
The Controller Loop вҖ” Heart of Kubernetes
  CONTROLLER LOOP (runs continuously)
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ                                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ OBSERVE  в”Ӯ вҶҗ Watch actual state       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ   "3 pods running"          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в–ј                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ COMPARE  в”Ӯ вҶҗ Compare to desired state в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (diff)   в”Ӯ   "spec says 5 replicas"   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в–ј                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   ACT    в”Ӯ вҶҗ Corrective action        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ(reconcile)в”Ӯ  "create 2 more pods"     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ loop forever в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

3.3 Worker Node Components вҖ” Deep Dive for Admins

Worker nodes run the actual application containers. Each node has three critical components you must maintain and troubleshoot.

a) kubelet вҖ” The Node Agent

рҹӨ– kubelet

рҹҺҜ Role
Agent on every node. Receives PodSpecs from API server, ensures containers are running and healthy, reports status back.
рҹ“Ӯ Config
/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
рҹ”Ң Port
10250 (HTTPS API)
вҡЎ Key Fact
kubelet is NOT a pod вҖ” it runs as a systemd service on the node. If it crashes, check with systemctl status kubelet.
рҹ”Қ Troubleshoot
journalctl -u kubelet -f вҖ” watch kubelet logs in real-time. Most common issue: certificate expired or runtime not responding.

b) kube-proxy вҖ” The Network Plumber

рҹҢҗ kube-proxy

рҹҺҜ Role
Maintains network rules on each node. Enables Service-based access to Pods вҖ” routes traffic to the right Pod endpoint.
вҡҷпёҸ Modes
iptables (default), IPVS (high performance, 1000+ services), nftables (newer kernel-based).
рҹ“Ұ Runs As
DaemonSet pod (one per node) in kube-system namespace. Check with kubectl get ds -n kube-system

c) Container Runtime

рҹҗӢ
containerd

Default for most K8s installations. CNCF graduated. Used by EKS, AKS, GKE, and kubeadm clusters.

рҹ”ҙ
CRI-O

Kubernetes-native, minimal runtime. Used by OpenShift. Versioned in lockstep with K8s releases.

вҡ пёҸ CKA Troubleshooting: Node NotReady?

When a node shows NotReady, check these in order:

  • systemctl status kubelet вҖ” is the node agent running?
  • journalctl -u kubelet вҖ” any error messages?
  • systemctl status containerd вҖ” is the container runtime running?
  • crictl ps вҖ” can the runtime list containers?

3.4 How a Pod Gets Created вҖ” The Full Journey

Understanding every step of pod creation is essential for CKA troubleshooting. When a pod fails, you need to know which step broke to fix it.

1
kubectl вҶ’ API Server

kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml sends HTTPS request to API server (port 6443) with TLS certificate authentication.

2
Authentication вҶ’ Authorization вҶ’ Admission

API server validates: Who are you? (certs/tokens) вҶ’ Can you do this? (RBAC) вҶ’ Is the YAML valid? (admission controllers)

3
API Server вҶ’ etcd

Validated Deployment object is persisted to etcd. The desired state is now recorded.

4
Controller Manager Creates Pods

DeploymentController sees new Deployment вҶ’ creates ReplicaSet вҶ’ ReplicaSetController creates Pod objects (still without a node).

5
Scheduler Assigns Nodes

Scheduler watches for Pods with nodeName: "" вҶ’ filters вҶ’ scores вҶ’ binds Pod to best node.

6
kubelet Starts Container

kubelet on the assigned node pulls the container image вҶ’ starts container via containerd вҶ’ sets up networking.

7
kube-proxy Updates Network

Network rules are updated so Services can route traffic to the new Pod's IP address.

8
Pod is Running вң…

kubelet reports Pod status вҶ’ API server вҶ’ etcd. The reconciliation loop continues monitoring.

Pod Creation Flow вҖ” Visual Summary
  kubectl apply в”Җв”Җв–¶ API Server в”Җв”Җв–¶ etcd (store)
                        в”Ӯ
                        в–ј
                 Controller Manager
                   (create Pods)
                        в”Ӯ
                        в–ј
                    Scheduler
                  (assign nodes)
                        в”Ӯ
                        в–ј
                 kubelet (on node)
                   (pull image)
                   (start container)
                        в”Ӯ
                        в–ј
                   kube-proxy
                (update network rules)
                        в”Ӯ
                        в–ј
                   Pod Running вң…
рҹ’Ў CKA Troubleshooting Map

Match the symptom to the failed step:

  • Connection refused вҶ’ Step 1 (API server down)
  • Forbidden/Unauthorized вҶ’ Step 2 (RBAC/certs)
  • Pod Pending вҶ’ Step 5 (scheduler can't find a node)
  • ImagePullBackOff вҶ’ Step 6 (kubelet can't pull image)
  • CrashLoopBackOff вҶ’ Step 6 (container starts but crashes)
  • Service unreachable вҶ’ Step 7 (kube-proxy/network issue)

3.5 Cluster Networking Overview (Admin Perspective)

Kubernetes networking has one fundamental rule: every Pod gets its own unique IP address, and all Pods can communicate with all other Pods without NAT. CNI plugins make this happen.

Kubernetes Networking вҖ” Three Layers
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  EXTERNAL TRAFFIC (Users, Internet)                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Access via: NodePort / LoadBalancer / Ingress       в”Ӯ
  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
  в”Ӯ  SERVICE NETWORK (ClusterIP range)                   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Virtual IPs managed by kube-proxy                   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  e.g., 10.96.0.0/12                                 в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Services вҶ’ route traffic to Pod endpoints           в”Ӯ
  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
  в”Ӯ  POD NETWORK (Pod CIDR)                              в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Real IPs assigned by CNI plugin                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  e.g., 10.244.0.0/16                                в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Every Pod gets a unique IP вҖ” flat network           в”Ӯ
  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
  в”Ӯ  NODE NETWORK (Physical/VM IPs)                      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Infrastructure network                              в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  e.g., 192.168.1.0/24                               в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

CNI Plugins вҖ” Your Choice as Admin

рҹҗҜ
Calico

Most popular. Full NetworkPolicy support. BGP routing. Used in the CKA exam environment.

рҹҗқ
Cilium

eBPF-based. Advanced observability. High performance. Growing rapidly in adoption.

рҹҹҰ
Flannel

Simple VXLAN overlay. Easy setup. No NetworkPolicy support. Good for learning.

рҹ•ёпёҸ
Weave

Encrypted mesh. Easy install. Auto-discovers. NetworkPolicy support.

рҹ“– CKA Exam: CNI Installation

After running kubeadm init, your cluster's Pods won't start until you install a CNI plugin. This is a common CKA task: kubectl apply -f https://docs.projectcalico.org/manifests/calico.yaml. Without a CNI, all pods (including CoreDNS) will be in Pending state.

3.6 Static Pods вҖ” Admin-Only Concept

Static Pods are managed directly by the kubelet on a specific node вҖ” not by the API server. They're how kubeadm deploys control plane components, and they're directly tested on the CKA exam.

рҹ“Ұ
Regular Pods

Created via API server (kubectl). Stored in etcd. Scheduled by the scheduler. Managed by controllers.

рҹ“Ң
Static Pods

Created by kubelet directly. YAML files in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. kubelet watches this directory and auto-manages pod lifecycle.

рҹ’Ў Control Plane = Static Pods!

On kubeadm clusters, the control plane components (API server, scheduler, controller-manager, etcd) are all static pods. This means you can edit their configuration by modifying the YAML files in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/:

terminal вҖ” control plane static pods 
# List control plane static pod manifests
ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# Output:
#   etcd.yaml
#   kube-apiserver.yaml
#   kube-controller-manager.yaml
#   kube-scheduler.yaml

# Static pods have nodename suffix
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep controlplane
# Output:
#   etcd-controlplane                  1/1   Running
#   kube-apiserver-controlplane        1/1   Running
#   kube-controller-manager-controlplane  1/1   Running
#   kube-scheduler-controlplane        1/1   Running

Creating a Custom Static Pod

static-nginx.yaml вҖ” /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: static-nginx
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
вҡ пёҸ CKA Static Pod Tips

Key facts for the exam:

  • Static pods can ONLY be created/deleted by adding/removing YAML from the manifest directory
  • You cannot delete a static pod with kubectl delete вҖ” kubelet recreates it immediately
  • Check the staticPodPath in /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml to find the manifest directory
  • Static pod names always end with -<nodename> (e.g., static-nginx-worker01)

рҹ”‘ Chapter 3 Key Takeaways for CKA

This is the most architecturally important chapter. Every troubleshooting scenario on the CKA requires you to know which component does what, where its logs are, and how to restart it. Memorize: API server (port 6443), etcd (2379/2380), kubelet (10250), kube-proxy (DaemonSet), static pods (/etc/kubernetes/manifests/).

etcd Restore вҖ” Complete CKA Procedure

Restoring etcd is one of the most commonly tested CKA tasks. Here's the full step-by-step procedure:

terminal вҖ” etcd restore (CKA exam skill) 
# Step 1: Restore snapshot to a new data directory
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# Step 2: Update etcd static pod to use the new data directory
# Edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml:
#   - Change hostPath volume from /var/lib/etcd вҶ’ /var/lib/etcd-restored

# Step 3: Wait for etcd to restart automatically (kubelet watches manifests)
#   kubelet detects the manifest change and recreates the pod

# Step 4: Verify the restore
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl get nodes
рҹҡЁ CKA etcd Restore Gotcha

The --data-dir flag must point to a new directory, not the existing one. Then you update the etcd Pod manifest to point to this new path. If you restore to the same directory, etcd may fail to start. Always create a new path like /var/lib/etcd-restored.

Component Quick Reference вҖ” Ports, Configs & Logs

Component Port Config / Manifest Logs Command
kube-apiserver 6443 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml kubectl logs kube-apiserver-* -n kube-system
etcd 2379 / 2380 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml kubectl logs etcd-* -n kube-system
kube-scheduler 10259 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml kubectl logs kube-scheduler-* -n kube-system
controller-manager 10257 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml kubectl logs kube-controller-manager-* -n kube-system
kubelet 10250 /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml journalctl -u kubelet -f
kube-proxy 10256 ConfigMap kube-proxy in kube-system kubectl logs kube-proxy-* -n kube-system
containerd вҖ” /etc/containerd/config.toml journalctl -u containerd
CoreDNS 53 ConfigMap coredns in kube-system kubectl logs coredns-* -n kube-system
рҹ’Ў CKA Pro Tip: Key File Paths to Memorize
  • /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ вҖ” static pod manifests (control plane)
  • /etc/kubernetes/pki/ вҖ” all cluster certificates (CA, server, client)
  • /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf вҖ” kubeconfig for the admin user
  • /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml вҖ” kubelet configuration
  • /var/lib/etcd/ вҖ” etcd data directory
  • /etc/cni/net.d/ вҖ” CNI plugin configuration
  • /opt/cni/bin/ вҖ” CNI plugin binaries

вҡ пёҸ CKA Gotcha: Architecture Traps (25% of Exam!)

  • Static Pods vs DaemonSets вҖ” Both run one pod per node, but static pods are managed by kubelet directly (no API server control), while DaemonSets are API objects. Static pods have a mirror pod in API with -<nodename> suffix
  • etcd is NOT a static pod everywhere вҖ” kubeadm clusters run etcd as a static pod, but external etcd clusters exist. The exam may present either topology
  • Scheduler vs Controller Manager вҖ” The scheduler only assigns pods to nodes. The controller manager handles replication, node lifecycle, endpoints, etc. Don't mix them up
  • kube-proxy modes вҖ” iptables mode (default) vs IPVS mode. The exam may ask you to check which mode is active: kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-proxy-*
  • CoreDNS вҖ” Runs as a Deployment (not DaemonSet) in kube-system. Its ConfigMap is called coredns. Exam troubleshooting often involves DNS failures

CKA Speed Tip: These one-liners save 2+ minutes per question:

kubectl get pods -n kube-system вҖ” instantly see control plane health
kubectl get nodes -o wide вҖ” node IPs, OS, container runtime, kernel version
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | grep -i etcd вҖ” find etcd endpoints
systemctl status kubelet вҖ” check kubelet on any node
journalctl -u kubelet -f вҖ” stream kubelet logs for real-time debugging

вҡЎ Architecture inspection commands: kubectl cluster-info вҖ” API server and CoreDNS endpoints
kubectl get componentstatuses вҖ” (deprecated but may appear on exam) health of scheduler, controller-manager, etcd
kubectl get events -A --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' вҖ” cluster-wide events sorted by time
kubectl top nodes вҖ” CPU/memory usage (requires metrics-server)
рҹ“қ Chapter 3 Practice Drill

Scenario: Your cluster's API server is unreachable. The kubelet is running. Diagnose and fix the issue.

Steps to practice:

  • 1. SSH to the control plane node
  • 2. Check crictl ps вҖ” is the kube-apiserver container running?
  • 3. Check /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml вҖ” syntax errors? Wrong flags?
  • 4. Check journalctl -u kubelet вҖ” does kubelet report manifest errors?
  • 5. Fix the manifest вҶ’ kubelet auto-restarts the static pod

Key insight: On the CKA, if kubectl doesn't work, go to the node and use crictl + journalctl.

Chapter 4: Core Kubernetes Objects вҖ” The Admin's Toolkit

Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments, Services, Namespaces вҖ” the objects you'll manage daily as a CKA administrator. These are the building blocks of every Kubernetes workload.

рҹҹў Beginner вҸұпёҸ ~2 hours рҹ“– Core Objects рҹҺҜ Foundation

рҹҺҜ What You'll Learn

  • вң… Pods вҖ” the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes
  • вң… ReplicaSets вҖ” self-healing pod management
  • вң… Deployments вҖ” rolling updates, rollbacks, scaling
  • вң… Services вҖ” stable networking for ephemeral pods
  • вң… Namespaces вҖ” logical cluster isolation
  • вң… Labels, Selectors & Annotations вҖ” the glue connecting everything
  • вң… Full object relationship map вҖ” how all objects interact
рҹҺ“ CKA Relevance
Workloads & Scheduling 15% Services & Networking 20%
Object Mastery = Exam Speed

Every CKA task involves core objects. You'll create Deployments, expose them with Services, troubleshoot pod issues, and manage namespaces. Master these objects imperatively (fast kubectl commands) and declaratively (YAML manifests).

4.1 Pods вҖ” The Smallest Unit

A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes вҖ” one or more containers sharing the same network namespace (IP) and storage volumes. You rarely create pods directly; you use Deployments. But you debug pods constantly.

Pod Anatomy вҖ” Single vs Multi-Container
  SINGLE-CONTAINER POD              MULTI-CONTAINER POD
  (most common)                     (sidecar pattern)

  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ         в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  POD                  в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  POD (shared network)         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  IP: 10.244.1.5       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  IP: 10.244.1.8               в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ                                в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ Container  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ Django в”Ӯ в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ Nginx  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ anihpj:v1    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ port:8000в”Ӯ в”Ӯ port:80  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ port: 8000   в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  localhost  в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ           в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ           в”Ӯ Volume в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ           в”Ӯ(shared)в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ           в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ          в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ         в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ’Ў
Key Pod Facts
  • Every container in a pod shares the same IP
  • Containers communicate via localhost
  • Pods are ephemeral вҖ” they die and get replaced, never repaired
  • As admin: you debug pods, not create them directly
рҹ”§
Essential Pod Commands
  • kubectl get pods -o wide
  • kubectl describe pod <name>
  • kubectl logs <pod> -c <container>
  • kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh
anihpj-pod.yaml вҖ” Pod manifest 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    tier: backend
spec:
  containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:v1
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "500m"

4.2 ReplicaSets вҖ” Maintaining Pod Count

A ReplicaSet ensures a specified number of identical pods are running at all times. If a pod dies, the ReplicaSet controller creates a replacement. You rarely create ReplicaSets directly вҖ” Deployments manage them for you.

ReplicaSet Self-Healing вҖ” Maintaining 3 Replicas
  ReplicaSet: anihpj-rs (replicas: 3, selector: app=anihpj)

  HEALTHY STATE:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ 1  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ 2  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ 3  в”Ӯ   3/3 Running вң…
  в”Ӯanihpjв”Ӯ  в”Ӯanihpjв”Ӯ  в”Ӯanihpjв”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  POD 2 CRASHES:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ 1  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ’Җ 2  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ 3  в”Ӯ   2/3 Running вҡ пёҸ
