  в”Ӯanihpjв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ DEAD в”Ӯ  в”Ӯanihpjв”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                в”Ӯ
                в”Ӯ  ReplicaSet: "I need 3, only 2 running!"
                в–ј
  SELF-HEALED:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ 1  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ 4  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ 3  в”Ӯ   3/3 Running вң…
  в”Ӯanihpjв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ NEW! в”Ӯ  в”Ӯanihpjв”Ӯ   (new pod created)
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ“– ReplicaSet vs Deployment

A ReplicaSet only ensures pod count. A Deployment adds rolling updates, rollback history, and version tracking on top. In practice, you always use Deployments вҖ” they create and manage ReplicaSets for you.

4.3 Deployments вҖ” Managing Application Lifecycle

Deployments are the standard way to run stateless applications. You declare the desired state (image, replicas, resources), and the Deployment controller makes it happen вҖ” including rolling updates, rollbacks, and scaling.

Deployment Ownership Chain
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  Deployment: anihpj                                    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җ manages в”Җв”Җв–¶ ReplicaSet (current: v3)             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ              в”ңв”Җв”Җ owns в”Җв”Җв–¶ Pod anihpj-7f8b4-abc1    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ              в”ңв”Җв”Җ owns в”Җв”Җв–¶ Pod anihpj-7f8b4-abc2    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ              в””в”Җв”Җ owns в”Җв”Җв–¶ Pod anihpj-7f8b4-abc3    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ                                                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җ keeps в”Җв”Җв–¶ ReplicaSet (old: v2, 0 replicas)       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җ keeps в”Җв”Җв–¶ ReplicaSet (old: v1, 0 replicas)       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Old ReplicaSets kept for rollback history!            в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
anihpj-deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        version: v3
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: anihpj
        image: anihpj:v3
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "128Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "500m"

Pod Lifecycle вҖ” States You Must Know for CKA

Pod Phase Transitions
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ Pending  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Running  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”ӮSucceeded в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ (exit 0) в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
       в”Ӯ               в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ               в–ј
       в”Ӯ          в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ  Failed  в”Ӯ
                  в”Ӯ(exit вү  0)в”Ӯ
                  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  STATUS COLUMN (what you see in kubectl get pods):
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ ContainerCreating в”Ӯ Image pulling, volumes mounting   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Running           в”Ӯ At least one container running    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Completed         в”Ӯ All containers exited with 0      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Error             в”Ӯ Container exited with error       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ CrashLoopBackOff  в”Ӯ Container keeps crashing, backoff в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ ImagePullBackOff  в”Ӯ Can't pull container image        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Pending           в”Ӯ Not yet scheduled to a node       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Terminating       в”Ӯ Pod is being gracefully deleted   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Init:N/M          в”Ӯ Init containers running           в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
вҡ пёҸ CKA Troubleshooting: Common Pod Statuses
  • Pending вҶ’ check: scheduler (no node fits), ResourceQuota exceeded, PVC not bound
  • ImagePullBackOff вҶ’ check: image name typo, private registry auth (imagePullSecrets), network
  • CrashLoopBackOff вҶ’ check: kubectl logs <pod> --previous for the crash reason
  • Init:0/1 вҶ’ check: init container stuck вҖ” kubectl logs <pod> -c <init-container>

Deployment Operations (CKA Speed)

terminal вҖ” deployment commands 
# Create deployment imperatively (CKA speed!)
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3

# Scale up/down
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=5

# Update image (triggers rolling update)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v4

# Check rollout status
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj

# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj

# Rollback to previous version
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj

# Rollback to specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=2
рҹ’Ў Rolling Update Strategy

maxSurge: 1 = allow 1 extra pod during update. maxUnavailable: 0 = never have fewer than desired replicas. This ensures zero-downtime deployments. The exam tests your ability to configure and troubleshoot rolling updates.

4.4 Services вҖ” Stable Network Endpoints

Pods get random IPs that change every time they restart. Services provide a stable IP and DNS name that routes traffic to healthy pods matching a label selector.

Service Type Access From Use Case Port Range
ClusterIP Inside cluster only Internal services (default) Any
NodePort External via node IP Dev/testing 30000-32767
LoadBalancer External via cloud LB Production (cloud) Any
ExternalName DNS alias (CNAME) External services N/A
Service Type Comparison вҖ” Visual
  ClusterIP (default)         NodePort                 LoadBalancer
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ        в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  Inside cluster  в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  Node:30080      в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ  Cloud LB       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  only            в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ  203.0.113.50   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ       в–ј          в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  svc:10.96.0.10 в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  svc:10.96.0.11 в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ       в–ј         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ  svc:10.96.0.12в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в–ј          в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ       в–ј          в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ       в–ј         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ в”Ӯpod в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ      в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ
                                                       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
anihpj-service.yaml вҖ” ClusterIP + NodePort 
# ClusterIP (internal)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj          # matches pods with label app=anihpj
  ports:
  - port: 80             # service port
    targetPort: 8000     # pod/container port
  type: ClusterIP
---
# NodePort (external dev access)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-nodeport
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
    nodePort: 30080      # accessible at NodeIP:30080
  type: NodePort
terminal вҖ” CKA imperative service creation 
# Expose deployment as ClusterIP (fast!)
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000

# Expose as NodePort
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 \
  --type=NodePort --name=anihpj-nodeport

# Check service endpoints
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc

Kubernetes DNS вҖ” How Services Get Discovered

Every Service automatically gets a DNS name inside the cluster. CoreDNS resolves these names so pods can find each other without hard-coding IPs:

Kubernetes Service DNS Resolution
  Pod in anihpj-dev namespace:
    curl http://anihpj-svc                    вҶҗ same namespace
    curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-dev         вҶҗ explicit namespace
    curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-dev.svc     вҶҗ full service path
    curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-dev.svc.cluster.local  вҶҗ FQDN

  DNS Pattern:
  <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
  
  Cross-namespace access:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ         в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ NS: anihpj-dev       в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ NS: database         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ  DNS    в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Pod: frontend        в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ Service: postgres-svcв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ curl postgres-svc    в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ (ClusterIP)          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   .database.svc      в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   .cluster.local     в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ в”Ӯ рҹ“Ұ pg в”Ӯ             в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ         в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ             в”Ӯ
                                   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ’Ў CKA Tip: Debugging DNS Issues

If services aren't reachable by name, check CoreDNS: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns. Test DNS from inside a pod: kubectl exec -it <pod> -- nslookup anihpj-svc. CoreDNS logs: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns.

4.5 Namespaces вҖ” Virtual Clusters

Namespaces provide logical isolation within a cluster. As a CKA admin, you create namespaces, apply ResourceQuotas, set RBAC policies, and manage resources across them.

Namespace Isolation вҖ” anihpj Example
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  CLUSTER                                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ NS: anihpj-dev    в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ NS: anihpj-prod   в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹҹў 1 replica      в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ рҹ”ҙ 3 replicas     в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ DEBUG=True        в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ DEBUG=False       в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ limits: low       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ limits: high      в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ      в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ NS: kube-system   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ NS: monitoring    в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ рҹ”§ CoreDNS        в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ рҹ“Ҡ Prometheus     в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    kube-proxy     в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ    Grafana        в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ      в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Each namespace can have its own:                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  вҖў ResourceQuotas  вҖў RBAC Roles  вҖў NetworkPolicies        в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Default Namespaces

рҹ“Ұ
default

Where objects go if no namespace is specified. Don't use in production.

рҹ”§
kube-system

System components: CoreDNS, kube-proxy, metrics-server. Don't deploy apps here.

рҹҢҚ
kube-public

Readable by all (even unauthenticated). Contains cluster-info ConfigMap.

рҹ’“
kube-node-lease

Node heartbeat Lease objects. Used for node health detection.

terminal вҖ” namespace commands 
# Create namespace
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev

# List all namespaces
kubectl get namespaces

# Run commands in a specific namespace
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev

# Set default namespace for current context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj-dev

# List ALL pods across ALL namespaces
kubectl get pods -A
вҡ пёҸ CKA Exam Namespace Traps

The CKA exam gives tasks in different namespaces. Always read the namespace instruction at the top of each question. If you create a resource in the wrong namespace, you get zero marks. Use -n <namespace> or switch context with kubectl config set-context.

ResourceQuota вҖ” Limiting Namespace Resources

As a CKA admin, you enforce resource limits per namespace to prevent one team from consuming the entire cluster:

anihpj-quota.yaml вҖ” ResourceQuota 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: anihpj-quota
  namespace: anihpj-prod
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
    persistentvolumeclaims: "5"
рҹ’Ў LimitRange vs ResourceQuota

ResourceQuota limits the total resources for an entire namespace. LimitRange sets default and max limits for individual pods/containers. Use both together: ResourceQuota caps the namespace, LimitRange ensures no single pod is too greedy.

4.6 Labels, Selectors, and Annotations

Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects for organizing and selecting. They're the glue that connects Services to Pods, ReplicaSets to Pods, and NetworkPolicies to targets.

рҹҸ·пёҸ
Labels

For selection and grouping. Used by selectors to match objects. Keep them meaningful.

  • app: anihpj
  • tier: backend
  • env: production
  • version: v3
рҹ“қ
Annotations

For metadata and tools. Not used for selection. Store non-identifying info.

  • description: "Job portal backend"
  • owner: "team-alpha"
  • last-deployed: "2024-01-15"
  • prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
How Labels Connect Objects
  Service (selector: app=anihpj)
       в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ finds pods with matching labels
       в–ј
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ Pod           в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Pod           в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Pod           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ app: anihpj  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ app: anihpj  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ app: other   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ tier: backendв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ tier: backendв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ tier: frontendв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ вң… MATCHED    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ вң… MATCHED    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ вқҢ NOT matchedв”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
terminal вҖ” label commands 
# Add labels to existing pods
kubectl label pod anihpj-pod tier=backend

# Show labels
kubectl get pods --show-labels

# Filter by label selector
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
kubectl get pods -l "app=anihpj,tier=backend"
kubectl get pods -l "app in (anihpj, other-app)"

# Remove a label (trailing dash)
kubectl label pod anihpj-pod tier-

4.7 Kubernetes Object Relationship Map

This is the big picture вҖ” how every core object connects to form a working application deployment in Kubernetes.

How All Kubernetes Objects Connect
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ                                                             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Deployment в”Җв”Җmanagesв”Җв”Җв–¶ ReplicaSet в”Җв”Җownsв”Җв”Җв–¶ Pods         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ references                    labels   в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в–ј                                        в–ј            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  ConfigMap в—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ envFrom в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Pod spec в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Secret    в—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ envFrom в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Pod spec в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                        в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Service в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җselectorв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җmatchesв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ       в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ       в–ј                                                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Ingress в”Җв”Җroutes trafficв”Җв”Җв–¶ Service                        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  PVC в—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ volumeMount в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Pod spec                в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ                                                         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в–ј                                                         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  PV / StorageClass                                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Namespace в”Җв”Җcontains all aboveв”Җв”Җ                           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  RBAC в”Җв”Җcontrols access to all aboveв”Җв”Җ                      в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

рҹ”‘ Part 1 Key Takeaways for CKA

  • вң… Kubernetes вҖ” container orchestration: deployment, scaling, management
  • вң… Architecture вҖ” Control Plane (API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager) + Worker Nodes (kubelet, kube-proxy)
  • вң… API Server is the ONLY component that talks to etcd
  • вң… Core objects вҖ” Pod (smallest), Deployment (manages ReplicaSets), Service (networking), Namespace (isolation)
  • вң… Labels connect Services to Pods, ReplicaSets to Pods, and NetworkPolicies to targets
  • вң… Everything is declarative вҖ” tell K8s WHAT you want, not HOW
terminal вҖ” must-remember CKA commands 
# Cluster overview
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl api-resources

# Object inspection
kubectl get all -A
kubectl describe <resource> <name>
kubectl get <resource> -o yaml

# Speed commands (CKA exam)
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx                  # create pod
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx      # create deployment
kubectl expose deployment web --port=80          # create service

вҡ пёҸ CKA Gotcha: Core Object Traps

  • Pod vs Deployment вҖ” Never create bare pods on the exam unless specifically asked. A bare pod won't be rescheduled if the node fails. Use Deployments for self-healing
  • ReplicaSet selector must match template labels вҖ” If matchLabels doesn't match metadata.labels in the pod template, the RS won't manage those pods
  • Service selectors match pod labels, NOT deployment labels вҖ” A Service selects pods directly. If you change pod labels without updating the Service selector, traffic breaks
  • Namespace isolation вҖ” Services are namespace-scoped. To reach a service cross-namespace: <svc-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
  • kubectl run vs kubectl create вҖ” run creates a Pod; create deployment creates a Deployment. Don't confuse them

CKA Speed Tip вҖ” YAML Generation: Never write YAML from scratch on the exam. Use --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate templates:

kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx --replicas=3 --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml
kubectl expose deployment web --port=80 --target-port=8080 --type=NodePort --dry-run=client -o yaml > svc.yaml

вҡЎ Essential imperative commands for core objects: kubectl run mypod --image=nginx --port=80 --labels="app=web,tier=frontend"
kubectl create deployment myapp --image=nginx --replicas=3
kubectl expose pod mypod --port=80 --name=mypod-svc
kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas=5
kubectl set image deployment/myapp nginx=nginx:1.25
kubectl create namespace dev
kubectl label pod mypod env=prod
kubectl annotate pod mypod description="my pod"
рҹ“қ Chapter 4 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 5 minutes):

  • 1. Create namespace drill-ns
  • 2. Create a deployment web in drill-ns with image nginx:1.24, 3 replicas
  • 3. Expose it as a ClusterIP service on port 80
  • 4. Update the image to nginx:1.25
  • 5. Verify the rollout: kubectl rollout status deployment/web -n drill-ns
  • 6. Roll back to previous version: kubectl rollout undo deployment/web -n drill-ns
  • 7. Verify the image is back to nginx:1.24

Key insight: This drill covers 3 CKA domains вҖ” Workloads (create/scale), Services (expose), and Troubleshooting (rollout history).

рҹҹў Part 2: Getting Started вҖ” Hands-On Basics Chapters 5-8

Chapter 5: Setting Up Your Environment

Before you can manage a Kubernetes cluster, you need one to practice on. This chapter walks you through installing the tools, connecting to a cluster, managing multiple contexts, and getting your environment CKA-exam ready. Everything uses our anihpj/jobpost Django project as the target workload.

рҹҹў Beginner вҸұпёҸ ~1 hour рҹӣ пёҸ Setup рҹ’» Hands-On

рҹҺҜ What You'll Learn

  • вң… Choose the right local Kubernetes tool вҖ” Minikube, Kind, k3s, kubeadm, Docker Desktop
  • вң… Install and start Minikube with the optimal settings for CKA practice
  • вң… Install kubectl and verify it connects to your cluster
  • вң… Understand kubeconfig вҖ” the file that controls all cluster connections
  • вң… Switch between multiple clusters and contexts (directly tested on CKA)
  • вң… Verify cluster health and enable essential addons
рҹҺ“ CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Troubleshooting 30%
The CKA Exam Environment

On the CKA exam you'll be given multiple pre-built clusters and asked to switch between them. You must know kubectl config use-context and how kubeconfig works вҖ” it's tested in the very first instruction of most questions. Practice context switching until it's muscle memory.

  • Context switching вҖ” Every question starts with: "Use context: k8s-cluster-X". You must switch before answering.
  • kubectl is pre-installed вҖ” But knowing aliases and auto-completion saves critical exam time.
  • Bookmark kubernetes.io/docs вҖ” The only allowed reference during the exam.

5.1 Choosing a Local Kubernetes Tool

There are several tools to run Kubernetes locally. Each has trade-offs вҖ” the right choice depends on what you're practicing for:

Tool Best For Nodes Resources Install CKA Prep?
Minikube Learning, interactive 1 вҶ’ multi Medium Easy вң… Start here
Kind CI/CD, testing Multi Low Easy вң… Good
k3s Edge, lightweight Multi Very Low Easy вҡ пёҸ Differs from full K8s
kubeadm Real clusters Multi High Manual вҡ пёҸ вң… Must learn
Docker Desktop Windows/Mac dev 1 Medium Very Easy вҡ пёҸ Limited
рҹ’Ў CKA Learning Path Recommendation

Start with Minikube for Chapters 5-8 (instant setup, great for learning). Then switch to kubeadm in Part 3 (Chapter 9) to practice building clusters from scratch вҖ” this is directly tested on the CKA exam.

рҹ–ҘпёҸ What the CKA Exam Environment Actually Provides

Knowing what's pre-installed in the exam environment helps you practice in realistic conditions:

вң…
Pre-Installed Tools
  • вҖў kubectl with bash completion
  • вҖў jq for JSON processing
  • вҖў tmux for terminal multiplexing
  • вҖў curl and wget
  • вҖў vim / nano editors
  • вҖў systemctl for service management
рҹ“ҡ
Allowed References
  • вҖў kubernetes.io/docs вҖ” Official docs
  • вҖў kubernetes.io/blog вҖ” K8s blog
  • вҖў github.com/kubernetes вҖ” Source repo
  • вҖў In-browser terminal (no local apps)
  • вҖў One browser tab for docs only
  • вҖў No personal notes or bookmarks
вҡҷпёҸ
Exam Clusters
  • вҖў 6 pre-built clusters provided
  • вҖў Mix of single & multi-node
  • вҖў Different K8s versions possible
  • вҖў Must switch context per question
  • вҖў SSH access to nodes when needed
  • вҖў sudo available on nodes
вҸұпёҸ
Exam Format
  • вҖў 2 hours total duration
  • вҖў 15-20 questions (performance-based)
  • вҖў 66% passing score
  • вҖў Questions weighted differently
  • вҖў Can flag & return to questions
  • вҖў Free retake if first attempt fails
CKA Tool Progression
  Your CKA Journey:

  Stage 1 (Now)          Stage 2 (Part 3)       Stage 3 (Exam)
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  рҹҗЈ Minikube  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ  рҹ”§ kubeadm   в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ  рҹҺҜ Exam Env  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  1 command    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ  Full install в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ  Pre-built    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  start fast   в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ  manual setup в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ  clusters     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  learn basics в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ  real skills  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ  context switchв”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

5.2 Installing Minikube (Quick Start)

Minikube creates a single-node Kubernetes cluster inside a VM or container on your laptop. One command and you have a fully functional cluster.

Installation by OS

рҹӘҹ
Windows

choco install minikube
or
winget install minikube

рҹҚҺ
macOS

brew install minikube

рҹҗ§
Linux (x86)

curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-linux-amd64
sudo install minikube-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

рҹҗі
Driver

Requires Docker, VirtualBox, or Hyper-V. Docker is recommended.

Starting Your First Cluster

terminal 
# Start a cluster with optimal CKA practice settings
minikube start --driver=docker --cpus=2 --memory=4096

# Expected output:
# рҹҳ„  minikube v1.33.0 on Darwin 14.0
# вңЁ  Using the docker driver based on user configuration
# рҹ“Ұ  Preparing Kubernetes v1.30.0 on Docker 26.0.1...
# рҹ”—  Configuring bridge networking...
# рҹҸ„  Done! kubectl is now configured to use "minikube" cluster

# Check status
minikube status
# minikube
# type: Control Plane
# host: Running
# kubelet: Running
# apiserver: Running
# kubeconfig: Configured

# For multi-node practice (CKA-relevant):
minikube start --nodes=3 --driver=docker --cpus=2 --memory=4096
рҹ“– Multi-Node Practice

Use --nodes=3 when practicing node operations like kubectl drain, kubectl cordon, and node troubleshooting вҖ” these are heavily tested on the CKA. Single-node is fine for basic learning.

Minikube Architecture вҖ” What's Running Inside
  Your Machine (Host OS)
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ                                                                в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   Docker Engine / Hypervisor                                   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  Minikube Node (VM or Container)                       в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Control Plane в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  kube-apiserver    вҶҗ All kubectl commands go hereв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  etcd              вҶҗ Cluster state database      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  kube-scheduler    вҶҗ Assigns pods to node        в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  controller-manager вҶҗ Manages deployments, RS    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  CoreDNS           вҶҗ Internal DNS for Services   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Worker Components в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  kubelet           вҶҗ Runs pods on this node      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  kube-proxy        вҶҗ Network rules (Services)    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  containerd        вҶҗ Container runtime           в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                       в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”ӮPod 1в”Ӯ в”ӮPod 2в”Ӯ в”ӮPod 3в”Ӯ  вҶҗ Your workloads      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                       в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                                в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   kubectl (your laptop) в”Җв”ҖHTTPSв”Җв”Җв–¶ API Server :8443            в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  Key insight: Minikube runs BOTH control plane AND worker on the SAME node.
  Real clusters separate these onto different machines.

рҹ”§ Troubleshooting Common Minikube Issues

If Minikube doesn't start cleanly, try these fixes:

Error Likely Cause Fix
Exiting due to DRV_NOT_DETECTED Docker/Hyper-V not running Start Docker Desktop, then retry
insufficient memory Not enough RAM allocated minikube start --memory=2048 (lower)
Unable to connect to the server Cluster crashed or IP changed minikube delete && minikube start
apiserver not responding Stale cluster state minikube stop && minikube start
Slow startup (> 5 min) First-time image pull Normal вҖ” K8s images are large (~1 GB)
minikube-lifecycle.sh 
# Minikube lifecycle commands (know these)
minikube start         # Start cluster
minikube stop          # Stop cluster (preserves state)
minikube delete        # Destroy cluster (fresh start)
minikube pause         # Pause вҖ” free CPU but keep state
minikube unpause       # Resume paused cluster
minikube ssh           # SSH into the Minikube node
minikube ip            # Get the Minikube node IP address
minikube logs          # View Minikube system logs (for debugging)

5.3 Installing kubectl

kubectl is the CLI tool you'll use to interact with any Kubernetes cluster вҖ” local or remote, Minikube or production. Think of it as the "remote control" for your cluster.

installation-per-os.sh 
# Windows
choco install kubernetes-cli

# macOS
brew install kubectl

# Linux
curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"
sudo install kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl

# Verify installation
kubectl version --client
# Client Version: v1.30.0
# Kustomize Version: v5.0.4
How kubectl Connects to Your Cluster
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ         HTTPS (TLS)          в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  YOU             в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ  KUBERNETES CLUSTER в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ    ~/.kube/config tells        в”Ӯ                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  $ kubectl get   в”Ӯ    kubectl WHERE and WHO       в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ    pods          в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  API Server   в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ    1. Read kubeconfig           в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  :6443        в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ    2. Find server URL           в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  kubectl   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    3. Authenticate (cert/token) в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ    4. Send REST request         в”Ӯ          в–ј         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ    5. Receive JSON response      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    etcd       в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ kubeconfig в”Ӯ  в”Ӯв—Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ в”Җ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  (data store) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ    6. Format & display           в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                                 в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ’Ў CKA Speed Boost: Shell Aliases & Autocomplete

Set these up immediately вҖ” they save massive time on the exam:

~/.bashrc 
# Essential CKA aliases вҖ” add to ~/.bashrc
alias k='kubectl'
alias kg='kubectl get'
alias kd='kubectl describe'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'
alias kdel='kubectl delete'
alias kl='kubectl logs'
alias ke='kubectl exec -it'
alias kns='kubectl config set-context --current --namespace'

# Enable auto-completion (provided on CKA exam)
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

# Quick dry-run alias
alias kdr='kubectl --dry-run=client -o yaml'

# Verify aliases work
k get nodes

рҹ”Ң kubectl Plugins Worth Knowing

While not allowed on the CKA exam, these plugins accelerate daily work. Install via krew (the kubectl plugin manager):

Plugin What It Does Install Example
kubectx Fast context switching (interactive) kubectl krew install ctx kubectl ctx dev
kubens Fast namespace switching kubectl krew install ns kubectl ns anihpj-dev
neat Clean up -o yaml output (remove clutter) kubectl krew install neat kubectl get pod nginx -o yaml | kubectl neat
tree Show resource ownership hierarchy kubectl krew install tree kubectl tree deployment nginx

5.4 Understanding kubeconfig

The ~/.kube/config file is the single most important configuration file for kubectl. It tells kubectl where to connect, who you are, and which namespace to use by default. The CKA exam relies heavily on this.

kubeconfig вҖ” The 3 Building Blocks
  ~/.kube/config
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ                                                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  clusters:       вҶҗ WHERE to connect                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җ minikube    (https://192.168.49.2:8443)            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җ staging     (https://staging.k8s.example.com)      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җ prod        (https://prod.k8s.example.com)         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  users:          вҶҗ WHO you are                           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җ minikube-user   (client certificate)               в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җ dev-user        (token)                             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җ admin-user      (client certificate)               в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  contexts:       вҶҗ cluster + user + namespace            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җ minikube    (minikube + minikube-user)              в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җ dev         (staging + dev-user + anihpj-dev)      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җ prod        (prod + admin-user + anihpj-prod)      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  current-context: minikube   вҶҗ active connection         в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  Context = Cluster + User + Namespace
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  рҹ–ҘпёҸ Cluster  в”Ӯ + в”Ӯ  рҹ‘Ө User    в”Ӯ + в”Ӯ  рҹ“Ӯ Namespace в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  WHERE      в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  WHO       в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  SCOPE        в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
             \\          в”Ӯ          /
              в–ј          в–ј        в–ј
         в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
         в”Ӯ  рҹ“Ӣ Context = all three   в”Ӯ
         в”Ӯ  "dev" = staging +        в”Ӯ
         в”Ӯ  dev-user + anihpj-dev    в”Ӯ
         в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
~/.kube/config 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
current-context: minikube        # вҶҗ active context

clusters:
- cluster:
    certificate-authority: /home/user/.minikube/ca.crt
    server: https://192.168.49.2:8443
  name: minikube

users:
- name: minikube-user
  user:
    client-certificate: /home/user/.minikube/profiles/minikube/client.crt
    client-key: /home/user/.minikube/profiles/minikube/client.key

contexts:
- context:
    cluster: minikube
    user: minikube-user
    namespace: default           # вҶҗ default namespace for this context
  name: minikube
вҡ пёҸ CKA Exam Warning: Always Check Your Context

Every CKA question specifies which context to use. Running commands in the wrong context = wrong cluster = wrong answer = zero points. Always run kubectl config current-context before answering.

5.5 Managing Multiple Clusters and Contexts

In real environments (and on the CKA exam), you work with multiple clusters. Context management commands are your lifeline.

context-management.sh 
# List all contexts
kubectl config get-contexts
# CURRENT   NAME       CLUSTER    AUTHINFO        NAMESPACE
# *         minikube   minikube   minikube-user   default
#           dev        staging    dev-user        anihpj-dev
#           prod       prod       admin-user      anihpj-prod

# Show current context
kubectl config current-context
# minikube

# Switch to a different context
kubectl config use-context dev
# Switched to context "dev".

# Set default namespace for current context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj-dev
# Context "dev" modified.

# Create a new context
kubectl config set-context anihpj-staging \
  --cluster=staging \
  --user=dev-user \
  --namespace=anihpj-staging

# Merge multiple kubeconfig files
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:~/.kube/config-staging:~/.kube/config-prod
kubectl config get-contexts    # shows all merged contexts
рҹ“ӢRead Question
вҶ’
рҹ”„Switch Context
вҶ’
вң…Verify Context
вҶ’
вҢЁпёҸAnswer Question
рҹ’Ў Practice Exercise: Simulate CKA Context Switching

Create multiple contexts on Minikube to practice the exact workflow used on the CKA exam:

cka-context-practice.sh 
# Setup: Create 3 simulated contexts (all point to minikube)
kubectl config set-context cka-cluster1 \
  --cluster=minikube --user=minikube --namespace=default
kubectl config set-context cka-cluster2 \
  --cluster=minikube --user=minikube --namespace=kube-system
kubectl config set-context cka-cluster3 \
  --cluster=minikube --user=minikube --namespace=anihpj-dev

# в”Җв”Җ Simulate CKA Question 1 в”Җв”Җ
# "Task: Use context: cka-cluster1. List all pods in default namespace."
kubectl config use-context cka-cluster1
kubectl config current-context   # Verify: cka-cluster1
kubectl get pods

# в”Җв”Җ Simulate CKA Question 2 в”Җв”Җ
# "Task: Use context: cka-cluster2. Find kube-proxy pods."
kubectl config use-context cka-cluster2
kubectl config current-context   # Verify: cka-cluster2
kubectl get pods | grep kube-proxy

# в”Җв”Җ Simulate CKA Question 3 в”Җв”Җ
# "Task: Use context: cka-cluster3. Scale deployment anihpj to 3 replicas."
kubectl config use-context cka-cluster3
kubectl config current-context   # Verify: cka-cluster3
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=3

# Cleanup practice contexts
kubectl config delete-context cka-cluster1
kubectl config delete-context cka-cluster2
kubectl config delete-context cka-cluster3
kubectl config use-context minikube

5.6 Verifying Your Cluster

After setting up your environment, verify everything works. These are the same commands you'll use on the CKA to orient yourself in unfamiliar clusters.

cluster-verification.sh 
# 1. Cluster info вҖ” where is the API server?
kubectl cluster-info
# Kubernetes control plane is running at https://192.168.49.2:8443
# CoreDNS is running at https://192.168.49.2:8443/api/v1/.../kube-dns:dns

# 2. Node status вҖ” are all nodes Ready?
kubectl get nodes -o wide
# NAME       STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION   INTERNAL-IP     OS-IMAGE
# minikube   Ready    control-plane   5m    v1.30.0   192.168.49.2    Ubuntu 22.04

# 3. System pods вҖ” is the control plane healthy?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# NAME                               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# coredns-7db6d8ff4d-xxxxx           1/1     Running   0          5m
# etcd-minikube                      1/1     Running   0          5m
# kube-apiserver-minikube            1/1     Running   0          5m
# kube-controller-manager-minikube   1/1     Running   0          5m
# kube-proxy-xxxxx                   1/1     Running   0          5m
# kube-scheduler-minikube            1/1     Running   0          5m

# 4. API health check
kubectl get --raw=/healthz
# ok

# 5. All namespaces overview
kubectl get namespaces
# NAME              STATUS   AGE
# default           Active   5m
# kube-node-lease   Active   5m
# kube-public       Active   5m
# kube-system       Active   5m
1
cluster-info

Confirm API server URL is reachable

2
get nodes

All nodes should show STATUS: Ready

3
get pods -n kube-system

All control plane pods should be Running

4
healthz endpoint

API should return "ok"

рҹҡЁ CKA Troubleshooting: Node Not Ready?

If kubectl get nodes shows NotReady, use these commands to diagnose:

Symptom Check Command Common Cause
Node STATUS: NotReady kubectl describe node <name> kubelet stopped, network plugin down
Pods stuck in Pending kubectl describe pod <name> No schedulable nodes, resource limits
kube-system pods CrashLoop kubectl logs -n kube-system <pod> etcd disk full, config errors
API server unreachable systemctl status kubelet (on node) kubelet service not running

5.7 Enabling Essential Addons

Minikube addons extend your cluster with features that are standard in production. Enable these for a realistic practice environment:

Addon What It Enables CKA Relevant? Command
metrics-server CPU/memory metrics, kubectl top вң… Yes minikube addons enable metrics-server
ingress NGINX Ingress controller for HTTP routing вң… Yes minikube addons enable ingress
dashboard Web UI for visual cluster management вҡ пёҸ Not tested minikube addons enable dashboard
storage-provisioner Dynamic PV provisioning вң… Yes Enabled by default
enable-addons.sh 
# Enable essential addons for CKA practice
minikube addons enable metrics-server
minikube addons enable ingress

# Verify addons are running
minikube addons list | grep enabled
# |  ingress          | minikube | enabled вң… |
# |  metrics-server   | minikube | enabled вң… |
# |  storage-provisioner | minikube | enabled вң… |

# Verify metrics-server is working (wait ~60 seconds after enabling)
kubectl top nodes
# NAME       CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
# minikube   250m         12%    1024Mi          25%

рҹ“Ӣ Complete Environment Readiness Checklist

Run this one-shot script to verify your entire environment is ready for CKA practice:

environment-readiness-check.sh 
#!/bin/bash
echo "в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җ"
echo "  CKA Environment Readiness Check"
echo "в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җ"

# 1. kubectl installed?
echo -n "вң“ kubectl: "; kubectl version --client --short 2>/dev/null || echo "вқҢ NOT INSTALLED"

# 2. Cluster reachable?
echo -n "вң“ Cluster: "; kubectl cluster-info 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "вқҢ NOT REACHABLE"

# 3. Nodes ready?
echo "вң“ Nodes:"
kubectl get nodes -o wide 2>/dev/null || echo "  вқҢ Cannot list nodes"

# 4. Control plane pods running?
echo "вң“ Control Plane Components:"
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o custom-columns=\
"NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,RESTARTS:.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount" \
2>/dev/null | grep -E "etcd|apiserver|scheduler|controller"

# 5. DNS working?
echo -n "вң“ CoreDNS: "
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --no-headers 2>/dev/null | \
awk '{print $3}' || echo "вқҢ"

# 6. Metrics server?
echo -n "вң“ Metrics: "
kubectl top nodes 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "вҡ пёҸ Not enabled (run: minikube addons enable metrics-server)"

# 7. Alias configured?
echo -n "вң“ Alias 'k': "
type k 2>/dev/null && echo "configured" || echo "вҡ пёҸ Add: alias k='kubectl'"

# 8. Bash completion?
echo -n "вң“ Completion: "
complete -p kubectl 2>/dev/null && echo "active" || echo "вҡ пёҸ Run: source <(kubectl completion bash)"

echo "в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җ"
echo "  All checks complete!"
echo "в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җ"
рҹ“– Chapter 5 Complete вҖ” Your Environment is Ready!

You now have a working Kubernetes cluster with Minikube, kubectl configured with auto-completion, and essential addons enabled. You understand kubeconfig and can switch between contexts. Next: mastering the kubectl commands you'll use every day.

вҡ пёҸ CKA Gotcha: Environment Setup Traps

  • Context switching вҖ” The CKA exam gives you multiple clusters. You MUST switch context before answering each question: kubectl config use-context <name>. Forgetting this = zero marks for that question
  • Namespace scope вҖ” Many questions specify a namespace. If you forget -n <namespace>, you'll create objects in default and lose marks
  • kubectl autocompletion вҖ” Pre-configured on the exam, but verify with kubectl <TAB>. If broken: source <(kubectl completion bash)
  • Minikube вү  kubeadm вҖ” The CKA uses kubeadm clusters, NOT Minikube. kubeadm has real systemd services, real certificates, and real multi-node topology

CKA Speed Tip: Set these aliases at the START of your exam (they're usually pre-set but verify):
alias k=kubectl
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"
Now you can type: k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml

вҡЎ Context & config commands you'll use every question: kubectl config get-contexts вҖ” list all available clusters
kubectl config use-context <name> вҖ” switch to the right cluster
kubectl config current-context вҖ” confirm which cluster you're on
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=<ns> вҖ” set default namespace

Chapter 6: kubectl вҖ” The Administrator's Command Center

kubectl is the single tool you'll use for every CKA exam question and every day as a Kubernetes administrator. This chapter breaks down the syntax, the essential commands, output formatting, and the critical difference between imperative and declarative approaches.

рҹҹў Beginner вҸұпёҸ ~1.5 hours вҢЁпёҸ CLI рҹҺҜ CKA Essential

рҹҺҜ What You'll Learn

  • вң… The kubectl command anatomy вҖ” verb + resource + name + flags
  • вң… Essential admin commands organized by category (get, describe, create, debug)
  • вң… kubectl explain вҖ” your built-in API documentation during the exam
  • вң… Imperative vs Declarative вҖ” when to use each and the dry-run trick
  • вң… Output formatting with JSONPath, custom-columns, and label selectors
рҹҺ“ CKA Relevance
All Domains 100%
kubectl Speed = CKA Pass/Fail

The CKA is a timed, hands-on exam. You have 2 hours for 15-20 tasks. Every second counts. Administrators who type full resource names lose time. Administrators who know --dry-run=client -o yaml finish early. This chapter is the most important for exam speed.

  • Use aliases вҖ” k instead of kubectl saves ~8 characters per command
  • Use kubectl explain вҖ” faster than searching docs.kubernetes.io
  • Use imperative + dry-run вҖ” generate YAML skeletons in seconds

6.1 kubectl Syntax Pattern

Every kubectl command follows the same anatomy. Learning this pattern once lets you construct any command from memory:

kubectl Command Anatomy
  kubectl     get       pods      anihpj-xyz     -n anihpj-dev
  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ    в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ    в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ    в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ    в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
  command    VERB     RESOURCE     NAME            FLAGS

  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  VERB (what to do):                                        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  get, describe, create, apply, delete, edit, logs, exec,   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  top, patch, label, annotate, scale, rollout, drain,       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  cordon, uncordon, taint, auth, explain, port-forward      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  RESOURCE (what to act on):                                в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  pods (po), deployments (deploy), services (svc),          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  nodes (no), namespaces (ns), configmaps (cm),             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  secrets, ingress (ing), pv, pvc, sa, roles, clusterroles  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  FLAGS (modifiers):                                        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  -n namespace, -o output, -l labels, --all-namespaces (-A),в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  --sort-by, --field-selector, -w (watch), -f (file/follow) в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ’Ў Resource Short Names Save Time

Use abbreviations on the CKA: po=pods, deploy=deployments, svc=services, no=nodes, ns=namespaces, cm=configmaps, ing=ingress, pv=persistentvolumes, pvc=persistentvolumeclaims, sa=serviceaccounts. Find all with kubectl api-resources.

6.2 Essential Admin Commands вҖ” Daily Use

These are the commands you'll use every single day as a Kubernetes administrator. They're organized by what you're trying to do:

рҹ“Ӣ Get Information

get-commands.sh 
# List resources
kubectl get nodes -o wide              # nodes with IP, OS, runtime
kubectl get pods -A                     # ALL pods in ALL namespaces
kubectl get pods -o yaml                # full YAML definition
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.status.startTime  # sorted by start time
kubectl get all -n anihpj-dev           # everything in namespace

# Resource usage (requires metrics-server)
kubectl top nodes                       # node CPU/memory usage
kubectl top pods -n anihpj-dev          # pod CPU/memory usage

рҹ”Қ Inspect Details

describe-commands.sh 
# Detailed info (events, conditions, volumes, etc.)
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xyz -n anihpj-dev
kubectl describe node worker-1
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp -n anihpj-dev

вңҸпёҸ Create and Modify

create-modify-commands.sh 
# Apply manifests
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev

# Edit live objects
kubectl edit deployment anihpj -n anihpj-dev
kubectl patch deployment anihpj -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}' -n anihpj-dev

# Labels and taints (CKA-important)
kubectl label node worker-1 disk=ssd
kubectl taint node worker-1 key=value:NoSchedule

рҹ—‘пёҸ Delete and Clean Up

delete-commands.sh 
kubectl delete pod anihpj-xyz -n anihpj-dev
kubectl delete -f deployment.yaml
kubectl delete pods --field-selector status.phase=Failed -n anihpj-dev

рҹҗӣ Debug and Troubleshoot

debug-commands.sh 
# Logs
kubectl logs anihpj-xyz -n anihpj-dev
kubectl logs anihpj-xyz --previous -n anihpj-dev   # crashed container logs

# Interactive shell
kubectl exec -it anihpj-xyz -n anihpj-dev -- /bin/sh

# Port forwarding
kubectl port-forward pod/anihpj-xyz 8000:8000 -n anihpj-dev

# Debug a node (creates ephemeral debug pod)
kubectl debug node/worker-1 --image=busybox
рҹ“Ӣ
get

List resources. Add -o wide for extra detail.

рҹ”Қ
describe

Deep dive. Shows events, conditions, volumes.

рҹ“қ
logs

Container output. Add --previous for crashes.

рҹҗҡ
exec -it

Shell into a running container for debugging.

рҹҸ·пёҸ Labels & Selectors вҖ” The Glue of Kubernetes

Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects. Selectors use them to find and group resources. Services find pods by labels. Deployments manage pods by labels. Understanding this linkage is critical:

labels-and-selectors.sh 
# Add a label to a pod
kubectl label pod anihpj-xyz app=anihpj -n anihpj-dev

# Show labels
kubectl get pods --show-labels -n anihpj-dev
# NAME           READY   STATUS    LABELS
# anihpj-abc12   1/1     Running   app=anihpj,version=v1

# Filter by label (equality)
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj

# Filter by label (set-based)
kubectl get pods -l 'app in (anihpj, nginx)'
kubectl get pods -l 'app notin (redis)'
kubectl get pods -l 'environment'              # has label
kubectl get pods -l '!environment'             # doesn't have label

# Remove a label (trailing minus sign)
kubectl label pod anihpj-xyz version- -n anihpj-dev

# Overwrite a label
kubectl label pod anihpj-xyz version=v2 --overwrite -n anihpj-dev
How Labels Connect Everything
  Deployment                  Service                    Ingress
  selector:                   selector:                  backend:
    matchLabels:                app: anihpj                service:
      app: anihpj                                           name: anihpj-svc
       в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ   Labels are the          в”Ӯ
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                   в”Ӯ
                   в–ј
       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ  Pod                      в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ  labels:                  в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ    app: anihpj  в—Җв”Җв”Җ match  в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ    version: v1            в”Ӯ
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  If labels don't match вҶ’ Service has 0 endpoints вҶ’ App unreachable!

вҡЎ Top 15 CKA Must-Know Commands вҖ” Quick Reference

These commands appear in nearly every CKA question. Memorize them:

# Task Command
1 Switch context kubectl config use-context <name>
2 Set default namespace kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=<ns>
3 Generate YAML from command kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml
4 Create deployment kubectl create deploy <name> --image=<img> --replicas=<n>
5 Expose as service kubectl expose deploy <name> --port=80 --target-port=8000
6 Scale deployment kubectl scale deploy <name> --replicas=5
7 View pod logs kubectl logs <pod> [-c container] [--previous]
8 Shell into pod kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh
9 Get all in namespace kubectl get all -n <namespace>
10 Describe resource kubectl describe <resource> <name> -n <ns>
11 Find field paths kubectl explain <resource>.spec --recursive
12 Drain a node kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
13 Cordon/uncordon node kubectl cordon <node> / kubectl uncordon <node>
14 Rollout status/undo kubectl rollout status deploy/<name> / kubectl rollout undo
15 Apply a manifest kubectl apply -f <file.yaml>

6.3 kubectl explain вҖ” Built-in API Documentation

kubectl explain is your best friend during the CKA exam. It shows you the exact fields available for any resource вҖ” no internet search needed. The exam allows access to kubernetes.io/docs, but explain is often faster.

kubectl-explain.sh 
# Top-level fields for a resource
kubectl explain pod
# KIND:     Pod
# VERSION:  v1
# FIELDS:   apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec, status

# Drill into nested fields
kubectl explain pod.spec
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources.limits

# See ALL fields recursively (great for finding field paths)
kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy --recursive
# strategy     <Object>
#    rollingUpdate     <Object>
#       maxSurge       <string>
#       maxUnavailable <string>
#    type              <string>

# Find specific field names
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe --recursive
kubectl explain вҖ” Drill-Down Navigation
  kubectl explain pod
       в”Ӯ
       в–ј
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ apiVersion       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ kind             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ metadata в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв–¶ kubectl explain pod.metadata
  в”Ӯ spec в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв–¶ kubectl explain pod.spec
  в”Ӯ status           в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ         в–ј
                         в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                         в”Ӯ containers в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв–¶ kubectl explain pod.spec.containers
                         в”Ӯ volumes              в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
                         в”Ӯ nodeName             в”Ӯ         в–ј
                         в”Ӯ serviceAccountName   в”Ӯ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                         в”Ӯ tolerations          в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ image             в”Ӯ
                         в”Ӯ nodeSelector         в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ ports             в”Ӯ
                         в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ env               в”Ӯ
                                                     в”Ӯ resources в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв–¶ .resources.limits
                                                     в”Ӯ volumeMounts      в”Ӯ
                                                     в”Ӯ livenessProbe     в”Ӯ
                                                     в”Ӯ readinessProbe    в”Ӯ
                                                     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
рҹ’Ў CKA Pro Tip: Explain + Dry-Run = Speed

Forgot the field name for resource limits? kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources --recursive in 2 seconds. Then use kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate the skeleton and add the field. This combo is faster than searching docs.

6.4 Imperative vs Declarative вҖ” When to Use Each

Kubernetes supports two approaches to managing resources. Understanding when to use each is critical for both the CKA exam and production work.

Imperative vs Declarative вҖ” Two Approaches
  рҹҸғ IMPERATIVE: "Do this NOW"               рҹ“қ DECLARATIVE: "Make it like THIS"
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ $ kubectl run anihpj \            в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ $ kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ     --image=anihpj:v1             в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ $ kubectl scale deploy anihpj \   в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ deploy.yaml defines desired stateв”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ     --replicas=5                  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ K8s figures out what to change   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ $ kubectl expose deploy anihpj \  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ     --port=80                     в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ Like ordering from a menu:       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ "I want pasta" вҖ” kitchen handles в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Like giving step-by-step cooking  в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ the recipe                       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ instructions to a chef            в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                                    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ вң… Fast for one-off tasks         в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ вң… Repeatable, version-controlled в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ вң… Great for CKA exam speed       в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ вң… Production standard            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ вқҢ No record of what you did      в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ вң… GitOps friendly                в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ вқҢ Hard to reproduce              в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ вқҢ Slower for quick tests         в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Approach When to Use CKA Exam Production
Imperative Quick tasks, generate YAML вң… Use for speed вқҢ Avoid
Declarative Real environments, repeatable вң… Required for complex tasks вң… Always

The CKA Power Move: Imperative + Dry-Run = YAML Generator

dry-run-yaml-generators.sh 
# Generate Pod YAML (don't create, just output)
kubectl run anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --port=8000 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml

# Generate Deployment YAML
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > deployment.yaml

# Generate Service YAML
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 \
  --type=ClusterIP --dry-run=client -o yaml > service.yaml

# Generate Namespace YAML
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > namespace.yaml

# Generate ConfigMap YAML
kubectl create configmap anihpj-config \
  --from-literal=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > configmap.yaml

# Then edit the generated YAML and apply:
vim deployment.yaml   # add resource limits, env vars, etc.
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
вҡ пёҸ CKA Exam Critical: --dry-run=client vs --dry-run=server

--dry-run=client validates locally (no API call), always use this for YAML generation. --dry-run=server validates against the API server (checks if resource already exists, validates references). Use server for pre-apply validation.

рҹҺҜ Real CKA Scenario: "Create a Deployment with Specific Requirements"

The exam gives you constraints: name, image, replicas, port, namespace, labels. Here's the fastest workflow:

cka-scenario-workflow.sh 
# STEP 1: Generate the skeleton (5 sec)
kubectl create deployment web-app \
  --image=nginx:1.25 \
  --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > web-app.yaml

# STEP 2: Edit to add requirements (30 sec)
# vim web-app.yaml
# вҶ’ Add resource limits, nodeSelector, labels, etc.

# STEP 3: Validate before applying (5 sec)
kubectl apply -f web-app.yaml --dry-run=server
# deployment.apps/web-app created (server dry run)

# STEP 4: Apply for real (2 sec)
kubectl apply -f web-app.yaml

# STEP 5: Verify (5 sec)
kubectl get deployment web-app -o wide
kubectl get pods -l app=web-app

# Total: ~47 seconds вҶ’ vs 3+ minutes writing YAML from scratch!
вҡЎGenerate
вҶ’
вңҸпёҸEdit
вҶ’
вң…Validate
вҶ’
рҹҡҖApply
вҶ’
рҹ”ҚVerify

6.5 Output Formatting and Filtering

The CKA frequently asks you to extract specific information вҖ” pod names, node IPs, container images. Mastering output formatting separates fast solvers from slow ones.

Flag Purpose Example
-o wide Extra columns (IP, node, etc.) kubectl get pods -o wide
-o yaml Full object as YAML kubectl get pod anihpj -o yaml
-o json Full object as JSON kubectl get pod anihpj -o json
-o jsonpath Extract specific fields kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
-o custom-columns Custom table output kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase
--sort-by Sort output by field kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
-l key=value Filter by label kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
--field-selector Filter by field value kubectl get pods --field-selector status.phase=Running

JSONPath Examples (CKA Favorite)

jsonpath-examples.sh 
# Get all pod names
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'

# Get pod names + their node assignments
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.nodeName}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get node internal IPs
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.addresses[?(@.type=="InternalIP")].address}'

# Get container images across all pods
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}' -A

# Custom columns вҖ” clean table output
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
STATUS:.status.phase,\
NODE:.spec.nodeName,\
IP:.status.podIP
рҹ’Ў JSONPath Cheat Sheet for CKA

{.items[*]} = all items, {.metadata.name} = specific field, {range .items[*]}...{end} = loop, {"\n"} = newline, {"\t"} = tab, [?(@.type=="InternalIP")] = filter. Practice these вҖ” JSONPath extraction is tested on every CKA exam.

рҹҺҜ Common CKA Output Extraction Patterns

These exact patterns appear on CKA exams. Save as muscle memory:

cka-extraction-patterns.sh 
# в”Җв”Җ Pattern 1: "List pod names sorted by creation time" в”Җв”Җ
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}'

# в”Җв”Җ Pattern 2: "Find which node each pod is running on" в”Җв”Җ
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=\
POD:.metadata.name,\
NODE:.spec.nodeName

# в”Җв”Җ Pattern 3: "Get the IP address of all nodes" в”Җв”Җ
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath=\
'{.items[*].status.addresses[?(@.type=="InternalIP")].address}'

# в”Җв”Җ Pattern 4: "List all container images in the cluster" в”Җв”Җ
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath=\
'{range .items[*]}{.spec.containers[*].image}{"\n"}{end}' | sort -u

# в”Җв”Җ Pattern 5: "Find pods not in Running state" в”Җв”Җ
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running

# в”Җв”Җ Pattern 6: "Count pods per namespace" в”Җв”Җ
kubectl get pods -A --no-headers | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# в”Җв”Җ Pattern 7: "Save output to file" (CKA often requires this) в”Җв”Җ
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' > /opt/answers/pod-names.txt
рҹ“Ө
Output to File

Many CKA questions say "save result to /opt/answers/file.txt". Always use > redirect. Double-check with cat after writing.

рҹ”—
Pipe Power

Combine kubectl with grep, awk, sort, wc -l. Example: kubectl get pods -A | grep -c Running counts running pods.

вҡ пёҸ CKA Gotcha: kubectl Traps

  • kubectl apply vs kubectl create вҖ” create fails if the object exists; apply creates or updates. On the exam, use apply for YAML files and create for imperative one-liners
  • -o wide vs -o yaml вҖ” wide shows node placement + IPs (great for verifying scheduling). yaml shows the full spec (great for editing)
  • jsonpath quoting вҖ” Use single quotes around the jsonpath expression: -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'. Double quotes break in bash
  • --all-namespaces vs -A вҖ” Identical. Use -A to save time. But remember: some resources are cluster-scoped (nodes, PVs, clusterroles) and don't need -A

CKA Speed Tip: Master these output shortcuts вҖ” they appear in nearly every question:
k get po -o wide вҖ” see which NODE each pod is on
k get po -o yaml | grep -A5 image вҖ” quickly find container images
k get po --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp вҖ” sort by age
k get po -l app=web вҖ” filter by label selector

вҡЎ kubectl power commands for the exam: kubectl explain pod.spec.containers вҖ” in-cluster docs (faster than browser)
kubectl api-resources | grep -i network вҖ” find the right resource type
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' -A вҖ” timeline of cluster events
kubectl diff -f manifest.yaml вҖ” preview changes before applying

Chapter 7: YAML and Kubernetes Manifests

Every Kubernetes object is defined in YAML. You can't pass the CKA without being able to read, write, and debug YAML manifests quickly. This chapter covers YAML syntax, the 4 required fields every manifest must have, finding the right apiVersion, multi-resource files, and validation.

рҹҹў Beginner вҸұпёҸ ~1 hour рҹ“қ YAML рҹҺҜ CKA Essential

рҹҺҜ What You'll Learn

  • вң… YAML syntax вҖ” key-value pairs, lists, nested objects, and the rules that trip people up
  • вң… The 4 required fields every Kubernetes manifest must have
  • вң… How to find the right apiVersion for any resource
  • вң… Combining multiple resources in one YAML file with --- separator
  • вң… Validating manifests with --dry-run and kubectl diff before applying
рҹҺ“ CKA Relevance
All Domains 100%
YAML Mistakes = Failed Tasks

The #1 reason candidates fail CKA tasks isn't lack of knowledge вҖ” it's YAML indentation errors. A single wrong indent breaks the entire manifest. Practice writing YAML by hand until it's automatic. On the exam, always use --dry-run=server before applying to catch errors.

  • Spaces only вҖ” tabs will break your YAML silently
  • 2-space indentation вҖ” Kubernetes convention
  • Colon + space вҖ” key: value not key:value

7.1 YAML Basics for Kubernetes

YAML ("YAML Ain't Markup Language") is a human-readable data format. Kubernetes uses it to describe desired state. Here are the three building blocks you need:

YAML Building Blocks
  1пёҸвғЈ KEY-VALUE PAIR (scalar):
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ name: anihpj                в”Ӯ  string
  в”Ӯ replicas: 3                 в”Ӯ  number
  в”Ӯ debug: true                 в”Ӯ  boolean
  в”Ӯ description: "Job platform" в”Ӯ  quoted string
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  2пёҸвғЈ LIST (sequence) вҖ” each item starts with "- ":
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ ports:                      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ - 8000          в—Җв”Җв”Җ item 1  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ - 8080          в—Җв”Җв”Җ item 2  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ - 443           в—Җв”Җв”Җ item 3  в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  3пёҸвғЈ NESTED OBJECT (mapping) вҖ” indent = nesting:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ metadata:       в—Җв”Җв”Җ parent  в”Ӯ  2 spaces per level
  в”Ӯ   name: anihpj  в—Җв”Җв”Җ child   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   labels:       в—Җв”Җв”Җ child   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ     app: anihpj в—Җв”Җв”Җ grandchild
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  вҡ пёҸ CRITICAL RULES:
  вң… Spaces only      (2 per indent level)
  вқҢ NO tabs          (YAML breaks silently!)
  вң… Colon + space    key: value
  вқҢ NOT              key:value  (missing space!)
  вң… Dash + space     - item
  вқҢ NOT              -item  (missing space!)
  вң… Use ---          to separate multiple documents
yaml-syntax-examples.yaml 
# Key-value pairs
name: anihpj
replicas: 3
enabled: true

# List of strings
skills:
  - Python
  - Django
  - Kubernetes

# List of objects
containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1
    ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
      - containerPort: 80

# Inline list (shorthand)
args: ["runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]

# Multi-line string (pipe = preserve newlines)
description: |
  anihpj is a Django job posting platform.
  It supports filtering by Skills, Location, and Author.

# Folded string (> = join lines)
summary: >
  This is a single paragraph
  even though it spans multiple lines.
рҹҡЁ Common YAML Gotcha: Indentation

These look similar but mean completely different things:

Mistake What You Wrote What K8s Sees Fix
Tab character [TAB]name: anihpj Parse error вҖ” YAML fails entirely Use spaces only
No space after colon name:anihpj Treats name:anihpj as a single string name: anihpj
Wrong indent level labels: at root level Top-level field instead of nested under metadata Indent 2 spaces under parent
String vs number replicas: "3" Error: string instead of integer replicas: 3 (no quotes)
Missing dash for list containers:\n name: django Error: expected list, got mapping containers:\n- name: django
Indentation Matters вҖ” Same Words, Different Meaning
  вң… CORRECT вҖ” ports is a field of the container:
  containers:
  - name: django
    ports:                    вҶҗ indented under the list item
    - containerPort: 8000

  вқҢ WRONG вҖ” ports is a sibling, not a child:
  containers:
  - name: django
  ports:                      вҶҗ NOT indented = separate field!
  - containerPort: 8000

  вң… CORRECT вҖ” labels nested under metadata:
  metadata:
    name: anihpj
    labels:
      app: anihpj

  вқҢ WRONG вҖ” labels at same level as metadata:
  metadata:
    name: anihpj
  labels:                     вҶҗ NOT nested = top-level field!
    app: anihpj

рҹ”„ YAML vs JSON вҖ” Why Kubernetes Uses YAML

Kubernetes actually stores everything as JSON internally. YAML is a superset of JSON вҖ” easier to write by hand, with comments support:

рҹ“қ
YAML (What You Write)
# Pod definition
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1

вң… Comments вҖў вң… Readable вҖў вң… Less typing

{ }
JSON (What K8s Stores)
{
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "kind": "Pod",
  "metadata": {"name": "anihpj"},
  "spec": {
    "containers": [{
      "name": "django",
      "image": "anihpj:v1"
    }]}}

вқҢ No comments вҖў вқҢ Verbose вҖў вң… Precise

рҹ“– Both are Valid for kubectl

kubectl apply -f pod.yaml and kubectl apply -f pod.json both work. However, always use YAML for writing manifests (readable, supports comments). Use JSON with -o json for extracting data programmatically.

7.2 The 4 Required Fields вҖ” Every Manifest

Every single Kubernetes object вҖ” Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Secret вҖ” requires exactly 4 top-level fields. Memorize these:

рҹ”Ң
apiVersion

Which API group? v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1

рҹ“Ұ
kind

What type of object? Pod, Deployment, Service

рҹҸ·пёҸ
metadata

Identity: name, namespace, labels, annotations

вҡҷпёҸ
spec

Desired state вҖ” THE MEAT. Different for every resource type.

anihpj-pod.yaml 
apiVersion: v1                    # вҶҗ 1. API version
kind: Pod                         # вҶҗ 2. Object type
metadata:                         # вҶҗ 3. Identity
  name: anihpj-django
  namespace: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: web
    environment: dev
spec:                             # вҶҗ 4. Desired state
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: "anihpj.settings"
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "128Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "256Mi"
Manifest Structure вҖ” Annotated
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  apiVersion: apps/v1     вҶҗ Which API group?          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  kind: Deployment        вҶҗ What type of object?      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  metadata:               вҶҗ Identity (name, labels)   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ    name: anihpj                                      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ    namespace: anihpj-dev                             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ    labels:                                           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ      app: anihpj                                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  spec:                   вҶҗ Desired state (THE MEAT)  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ    replicas: 3              вҶҗ How many pods?          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ    selector:                вҶҗ Which pods to manage?   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ      matchLabels:                                     в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ        app: anihpj                                    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ    template:                вҶҗ Pod template            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ      metadata:                                        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ        labels:                                        в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ          app: anihpj        вҶҗ MUST match selector!    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ      spec:                  вҶҗ Pod spec (containers)   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ        containers:                                    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ        - name: django                                 в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ          image: anihpj:v1                             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ          ports:                                       в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ          - containerPort: 8000                        в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

7.3 Finding the Right apiVersion

Wrong apiVersion = manifest rejected by the API server. Here's the complete reference and how to look it up:

Resource apiVersion Kind Short Name
Podv1Podpo
Servicev1Servicesvc
ConfigMapv1ConfigMapcm
Secretv1SecretвҖ”
Namespacev1Namespacens
PersistentVolumev1PersistentVolumepv
PersistentVolumeClaimv1PersistentVolumeClaimpvc
ServiceAccountv1ServiceAccountsa
Deploymentapps/v1Deploymentdeploy
ReplicaSetapps/v1ReplicaSetrs
DaemonSetapps/v1DaemonSetds
StatefulSetapps/v1StatefulSetsts
Ingressnetworking.k8s.io/v1Ingressing
NetworkPolicynetworking.k8s.io/v1NetworkPolicynetpol
ClusterRolerbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1ClusterRoleвҖ”
ClusterRoleBindingrbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1ClusterRoleBindingвҖ”
find-apiversion.sh 
# List ALL resources with their apiVersion and short names
kubectl api-resources
# NAME               SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION                     NAMESPACED   KIND
# pods               po           v1                             true         Pod
# services           svc          v1                             true         Service
# deployments        deploy       apps/v1                        true         Deployment
# ingresses          ing          networking.k8s.io/v1           true         Ingress

# List all available API versions
kubectl api-versions
# apps/v1
# networking.k8s.io/v1
# rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
# v1

# Quick check: what apiVersion does Ingress use?
kubectl api-resources | grep -i ingress
# ingresses   ing   networking.k8s.io/v1   true   Ingress
рҹ’Ў CKA Memory Trick

Core resources = v1 (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, Namespace, PV, PVC, SA). Workloads = apps/v1 (Deployment, ReplicaSet, DaemonSet, StatefulSet). Networking = networking.k8s.io/v1 (Ingress, NetworkPolicy). RBAC = rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 (Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding).

API Group Hierarchy вҖ” Visual Map
  Kubernetes API Groups
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ                                                            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Core API (v1)  вҶҗ No group prefix                         в”Ӯ
